options.explanation: CT may delay needed surgery. Clinical peritonitis warrants surgical consultation first.
Peritoneal signs = surgical abdomen until proven otherwise. Get surgery involved immediately while resuscitating.

Indicates peritoneal irritation but not specific to perforation.
Non-specific inflammatory marker.

male-67-perforation.oldcartsNarrative: "The pain hit me like a knife about 4 hours ago. I was just sitting watching TV and suddenly - boom - worst pain of my life, right in my stomach. It spread everywhere. I can barely move - every movement makes it worse. I feel like I'm dying. I've had stomach problems for years, take a lot of ibuprofen (Advil) for my arthritis. My doctor wanted me to take something for my stomach but I never filled it. I feel hot and cold. Haven't been able to eat or drink anything."

historyQuestions.text: Describe exactly how the pain started.
historyQuestions.answer: Sudden. Like someone stabbed me. I can tell you exactly - 4 hours ago, 2pm, I was sitting in my chair and it just hit me. Worst pain of my life, instantly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Sudden onset 'thunderclap' abdominal pain = perforation, rupture, or ischemia until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Where is the pain now?
historyQuestions.answer: Started in my upper stomach but now it's everywhere. My whole belly hurts.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Generalized pain after focal onset = peritonitis from spreading contamination.

historyQuestions.text: Does moving make it worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes! I can't move at all. Even breathing hurts. The ambulance ride was agony.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Pain with movement = peritoneal irritation.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned ibuprofen (Advil) - how much do you take?
historyQuestions.answer: A lot. Probably 4-6 tablets a day for my arthritis. For years. My doctor told me to stop but it's the only thing that helps.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heavy NSAID use dramatically increases ulcer and perforation risk.

historyQuestions.text: Any stomach problems before today?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, burning in my stomach on and off. Sometimes worse after eating. Doctor said I might have an ulcer, gave me a prescription but I never took it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior dyspepsia/ulcer symptoms increase perforation probability.

historyQuestions.text: Any vomiting?
historyQuestions.answer: I've tried to vomit a few times but nothing comes up. Just dry heaving.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Retching without production can occur with severe pain and ileus.

historyQuestions.text: Any history of aneurysm or heart problems?
historyQuestions.answer: I have high blood pressure. They mentioned something about my aorta being a little big on a scan once, but said to just watch it.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Known aortic enlargement + sudden pain + hypotension = ruptured AAA until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Any irregular heartbeat or atrial fibrillation?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I don't think so. Just the blood pressure.
historyQuestions.reasoning: AFib is major risk factor for mesenteric embolism.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: BP 98/62 (hypotensive), HR 118 (tachycardic), RR 24 (tachypneic), Temp 101.2°F, SpO2 96%. SIRS criteria met.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Hypotension + tachycardia + fever = sepsis. This patient is in early septic shock.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly male lying completely still, knees drawn up. Appears acutely ill, diaphoretic, pale. Grimaces with any movement. Won't let you touch his abdomen initially.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic peritonitis posture - immobile, knees flexed to reduce peritoneal stretch.

physicalExam.finding: INSPECTION: Distended, not moving with respiration. AUSCULTATION: Absent bowel sounds. PALPATION: Board-like rigidity - involuntary guarding throughout. Diffusely tender. Rebound tenderness is severe.
physicalExam.reasoning: CLASSIC: 'Board-like rigidity' = peritonitis. This is a surgical abdomen. Involuntary guarding cannot be faked.

physicalExam.finding: Pulsatile mass not clearly palpable due to guarding and patient discomfort. Femoral pulses present bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: Must attempt AAA assessment though peritonitis may obscure. Pulses present argues against complete rupture.

physicalExam.finding: No gross blood. No melena. Hemoccult negative. Prostate normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: Bloody stool in mesenteric ischemia is late finding indicating bowel necrosis.

imaging.name: Upright Chest X-ray
imaging.result: FREE AIR under right hemidiaphragm (pneumoperitoneum). Crescent of air between liver and diaphragm is diagnostic for perforation.
imaging.reasoning: FIRST-LINE for suspected perforation. Fast (minutes), portable, highly specific. Free air visible in 70-80% of perforations. If positive, diagnosis is made - proceed to OR. Don't delay for CT.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray series
imaging.result: Upright/decubitus films may show free air. Also assess for obstruction pattern or ileus.
imaging.reasoning: If CXR doesn't show free air, left lateral decubitus film is most sensitive. Also evaluates for bowel obstruction.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast
imaging.result: Pneumoperitoneum. Thickened gastric antrum with adjacent extraluminal air and inflammatory stranding. Free fluid in pelvis. No AAA rupture. No other pathology.
imaging.reasoning: Most sensitive for perforation (detects small amounts of free air). Localizes perforation site. BUT delays surgery - only if diagnosis unclear or need to rule out other pathology (AAA).

imaging.name: Bedside ultrasound (FAST exam)
imaging.result: Free fluid in Morison's pouch and pelvis consistent with peritonitis. Aorta measures 3.8 cm with no aneurysm or rupture. IVC collapsing with respiration (needs fluid).
imaging.reasoning: FAST is rapid bedside assessment. Rules out AAA rupture (critical DDx). Detects free fluid. Can sometimes see free air (enhanced peritoneal stripe). Assesses volume status.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.result: WBC 18,500 with 92% neutrophils, 8% bands (left shift). Hgb 13.2, Hct 40%, Plt 245,000
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis with left shift indicates acute inflammation/infection. Baseline Hgb for surgical planning. Thrombocytopenia would suggest DIC/sepsis.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 139, K 4.8, Cl 98, CO2 22 (low), BUN 28, Cr 1.4 (elevated from baseline 1.0), Glucose 142
labs.reasoning: Elevated Cr indicates poor perfusion (pre-renal AKI). Low bicarb suggests metabolic acidosis from sepsis. Electrolytes for fluid resuscitation planning.

labs.name: Serum Lactate
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL marker of tissue hypoperfusion. Elevated lactate confirms sepsis/shock physiology. Trend lactate to assess resuscitation response. Lactate >4 = severe sepsis.

labs.name: Arterial Blood Gas
labs.result: pH 7.32, pCO2 32, pO2 85, HCO3 18, lactate 3.8 - metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation
labs.reasoning: Confirms metabolic acidosis. Assesses oxygenation. pH <7.35 with elevated lactate is concerning.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out acute pancreatitis as cause of acute abdomen. Pancreatitis can present similarly but has different management.

labs.name: Liver Function Tests
labs.result: AST 45, ALT 38, Alk Phos 85, T.bili 1.0 - normal
labs.reasoning: Rules out biliary pathology (cholecystitis, cholangitis). Baseline before surgery.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Coagulopathy in sepsis indicates DIC. Needed for surgical planning. This patient's normal coags are reassuring.

labs.name: Type and Screen
labs.result: Blood type A positive. Antibody screen negative. 2 units pRBCs crossmatched.
labs.reasoning: MUST have blood available for surgery. Intra-abdominal surgery has bleeding risk. Crossmatch if significant blood loss expected.

procedures.name: Nasogastric tube placement
procedures.result: 16Fr NG tube placed, 400mL bilious gastric contents returned. Connected to low intermittent suction.
procedures.reasoning: Gastric decompression reduces aspiration risk, may reduce peritoneal contamination. Mandatory before surgery.

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.result: Foley placed, initial output 150mL concentrated urine. Target UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr.
procedures.reasoning: Monitor urine output as resuscitation endpoint. Oliguria indicates inadequate perfusion.

treatments.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 30 mL/kg crystalloid bolus (LR or NS) given. Reassess perfusion, consider additional boluses or vasopressors.
treatments.reasoning: SEPSIS RESUSCITATION: 30 mL/kg crystalloid within first 3 hours per Surviving Sepsis guidelines. Don't delay for surgery - resuscitate AND operate.

treatments.name: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
treatments.result: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV + Metronidazole 500mg IV given immediately. Alternative: Ceftriaxone + Metronidazole, or Meropenem if severe.
treatments.reasoning: Cover gram-negatives, gram-positives, and ANAEROBES for GI perforation. Give within 1 hour of sepsis recognition. Source control (surgery) is equally critical.

treatments.name: IV analgesia
treatments.result: Morphine 4mg IV for pain control after surgical evaluation
treatments.reasoning: Adequate analgesia is humane. Old teaching to 'withhold pain meds to preserve exam' is outdated - treat pain after initial evaluation.

treatments.name: NPO
treatments.result: Nothing by mouth - patient going to OR
treatments.reasoning: Standard pre-operative preparation.

treatments.name: IV PPI
treatments.result: Pantoprazole 40mg IV given
treatments.reasoning: Acid suppression for peptic ulcer disease. Will need long-term PPI after surgery.

consults.name: General Surgery - EMERGENT
consults.result: Surgery consulted IMMEDIATELY upon recognition of peritonitis. Patient taken to OR within 2 hours of presentation.
consults.reasoning: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Don't wait for labs or imaging if peritonitis is clear. Call surgery FIRST. Graham patch (omental patch) is typical repair for perforated ulcer.

consults.name: Anesthesiology
consults.result: Anesthesia notified for emergent case. RSI planned given full stomach and aspiration risk.
consults.reasoning: Emergent surgery requires anesthesia coordination. Rapid sequence intubation for aspiration precautions.

consults.name: ICU for post-operative care
consults.result: ICU bed arranged for post-operative monitoring
consults.reasoning: Septic patient undergoing emergent surgery needs ICU-level care post-operatively.

disposition.name: Emergent operative intervention
disposition.result: To OR for exploratory laparotomy and Graham patch repair of perforated gastric ulcer
disposition.reasoning: ONLY appropriate disposition. Perforated viscus with peritonitis requires source control (surgery). Mortality increases with delay.

disposition.name: Conservative management
disposition.result: NOT APPROPRIATE - patient has peritonitis requiring surgery
disposition.reasoning: Non-operative management (Taylor's method) only for contained perforations in stable patients WITHOUT peritonitis. This patient has diffuse peritonitis - needs surgery.

actualDiagnosis.name: Perforated Peptic Ulcer with Peritonitis and Early Septic Shock
teachingPoints: Surgical abdomen = surgery consult FIRST, imaging second
Board-like (involuntary) rigidity is pathognomonic for peritonitis
Upright CXR can diagnose perforation in minutes - faster than CT
Resuscitate AND operate - don't delay surgery to 'stabilize'
Graham patch (omental patch) is typical surgical repair
NSAIDs + H. pylori = highest ulcer risk
Post-op: PPI, H. pylori testing and treatment, stop NSAIDs

male-67-perforation.teachingPoint: This is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY. On your surgery clerkship, when you see a patient with sudden severe abdominal pain who is lying completely still, afraid to move, with a rigid abdomen - that's peritonitis. Call your resident immediately. Don't wait for labs or CT. The mantra is: 'free air = free ride to the OR.' Management: NPO, NG tube, IV fluids, broad-spectrum antibiotics, and emergent surgery.
male-67-perforation.clinicalPearl: The surgery attending will ask you: 'What are the 5 causes of free air?' Answer: (1) Perforated peptic ulcer (most common), (2) Perforated diverticulitis, (3) Perforated appendicitis, (4) Perforated colon cancer, (5) Iatrogenic (recent endoscopy/surgery). Also know that 10% of perforations don't show free air on CXR - if clinical suspicion is high, get CT or go to OR anyway.

missedConsequences.Complete Small Bowel Obstruction: Progressive distension, aspiration risk, electrolyte derangements.

Adhesions cause 60-75% of SBO in developed countries. Always ask about surgical history.
More common in large bowel obstruction.

options.explanation: Expected in any bowel obstruction.
Suggests obstruction but not strangulation.

allDiagnoses.name: Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Prior surgery, classic symptoms, CT findings.

allDiagnoses.name: Strangulated SBO
allDiagnoses.rationale: Must assess for signs of bowel compromise.

allDiagnoses.name: Incarcerated Hernia

allDiagnoses.name: Large Bowel Obstruction

female-72-sbo.oldcartsNarrative: "I've been vomiting for 2 days. First it was what I ate, now it's this green bitter stuff. My belly is getting bigger and bigger. I'm passing a little gas but no bowel movement for 3 days. The pain comes in waves - it cramps up, then eases, then cramps again. I had surgery for a ruptured appendix 20 years ago, and a hysterectomy 15 years ago. I feel pretty miserable."

historyQuestions.text: What surgeries have you had?
historyQuestions.answer: Appendix removed 20 years ago - it had ruptured. Hysterectomy 15 years ago for fibroids. Knee replacement 5 years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Prior abdominal surgery = adhesions = #1 cause of SBO. Multiple surgeries increase risk.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the vomiting.
historyQuestions.answer: At first it was food. Now it's green/yellow liquid, very bitter. Keeps coming up.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bilious (green) vomiting indicates obstruction distal to ampulla of Vater - small bowel.

historyQuestions.text: Are you passing gas or having bowel movements?
historyQuestions.answer: I passed a little gas yesterday but nothing since. No bowel movement for 3 days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Obstipation (no flatus or stool) suggests complete obstruction. Partial obstruction may pass some gas.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the pain.
historyQuestions.answer: It comes in waves. Cramps up really bad for a minute, then settles down, then cramps again.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Colicky, crampy pain = peristalsis against obstruction. Constant severe pain suggests strangulation.

historyQuestions.text: Has your belly gotten bigger?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, much bigger. I look pregnant. My pants don't fit.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive distension from accumulating fluid and gas proximal to obstruction.

historyQuestions.text: Have you noticed any bulges in your groin or belly?
historyQuestions.answer: I don't think so. I haven't really looked.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Must examine for hernias - incarcerated hernia is surgical emergency.

physicalExam.finding: BP 108/68, HR 102, Temp 99.8°F. Mildly tachycardic, low-grade fever.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia from dehydration. Low-grade fever may indicate early ischemia or just inflammation.

physicalExam.finding: Uncomfortable elderly woman, shifting position. Appears dehydrated - dry lips, sunken eyes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Dehydration from vomiting and third-spacing.

physicalExam.finding: INSPECTION: Markedly distended. Visible peristalsis. Old surgical scars (RLQ, lower midline). AUSCULTATION: High-pitched, tinkling bowel sounds with rushes. PALPATION: Tympanitic to percussion. Mildly tender diffusely but NO peritoneal signs. No rebound, no guarding. HERNIA CHECK: No inguinal, femoral, or incisional hernias.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic SBO exam: distension, high-pitched sounds, tympany. IMPORTANTLY - no peritoneal signs argues against strangulation.

physicalExam.finding: Empty rectal vault. No masses. No stool in vault.
physicalExam.reasoning: Empty rectum supports diagnosis of obstruction.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray (supine and upright/decubitus)
imaging.result: Multiple dilated loops of small bowel (>3cm diameter). Air-fluid levels on upright film at different heights ('stepladder' pattern). Minimal colonic gas. No free air.
imaging.reasoning: First-line imaging for suspected SBO. Classic findings: dilated small bowel (>3cm), air-fluid levels, paucity of colonic gas. Fast, available, inexpensive. Sensitivity ~80% for SBO.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast
imaging.result: Dilated proximal small bowel to 4.5cm. Clear transition point in mid-ileum with adhesive band visualized. Decompressed distal small bowel and colon. No closed-loop obstruction. Good bowel wall enhancement (no ischemia). Small amount of free fluid (reactive). No free air.
imaging.reasoning: CT is GOLD STANDARD for SBO. Identifies: transition point, cause of obstruction, signs of strangulation (poor wall enhancement, mesenteric haziness, closed-loop), and complications. Sensitivity >95%.

imaging.name: CT signs of strangulation (NOT present here)
imaging.result: Would look for: decreased wall enhancement, mesenteric haziness/edema, 'whirl sign' (mesentery twist), closed-loop (C or U-shaped dilated loop), free fluid, pneumatosis
imaging.reasoning: CRITICAL to identify strangulation on CT - requires emergent surgery. This patient has NONE of these findings, which is reassuring.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.reasoning: Mild leukocytosis expected with obstruction. WBC >15-20K or marked left shift concerning for strangulation/necrosis. Elevated Hgb suggests hemoconcentration from dehydration.

labs.name: Basic Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 131 (low), K 3.1 (low), Cl 92 (low), CO2 30, BUN 32, Cr 1.3 (elevated), Glucose 108
labs.reasoning: Classic 'vomiting electrolytes': hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis. Elevated BUN:Cr ratio (>20:1) indicates pre-renal azotemia from dehydration. MUST correct before any surgery.

labs.name: Serum Lactate
labs.reasoning: CRITICAL: Normal lactate is VERY reassuring - strongly argues against bowel ischemia. Elevated lactate (>2.5) would be concerning for strangulation.

labs.name: Venous or Arterial Blood Gas
labs.result: pH 7.48, pCO2 45, HCO3 32 - metabolic alkalosis from vomiting/NG losses
labs.reasoning: Confirms metabolic alkalosis from GI losses. Guides electrolyte replacement.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis as cause of ileus/vomiting.

procedures.name: Nasogastric tube decompression
procedures.result: 16Fr NG tube placed, 800mL bilious output on initial placement. Connected to low intermittent suction.
procedures.reasoning: MANDATORY for SBO management. Decompresses stomach, reduces aspiration risk, may help resolve partial obstruction, therapeutic and diagnostic (ongoing high output suggests complete obstruction).

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.result: Foley placed, initial output 200mL concentrated urine. Target UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr.
procedures.reasoning: Monitor urine output as marker of resuscitation. Oliguric patients need more fluid.

procedures.name: Gastrografin (water-soluble contrast) challenge
procedures.result: Consider at 24-48 hours if no resolution. If contrast reaches colon within 24 hours, likely to resolve non-operatively. If no passage, surgery likely needed.
procedures.reasoning: Gastrografin is THERAPEUTIC (osmotic effect) and DIAGNOSTIC (predicts need for surgery). Give 100mL via NG, X-ray at 8 and 24 hours. Contrast in colon = 99% resolve non-op.

treatments.name: NPO (nothing by mouth)
treatments.result: Strict NPO - bowel rest essential for obstruction
treatments.reasoning: Bowel rest is fundamental to SBO management. No oral intake until obstruction resolves.

treatments.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: Lactated Ringer's bolus 2L, then maintenance at 150 mL/hr. Monitor UOP and reassess.
treatments.reasoning: Third-spacing and vomiting cause significant volume depletion. Aggressive resuscitation with isotonic crystalloid. Target UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr.

treatments.name: Electrolyte replacement
treatments.result: KCl 40 mEq IV in first liter (for K 3.1). Recheck in 4-6 hours. May need additional potassium and chloride.
treatments.reasoning: Hypokalemia and hypochloremia from vomiting/NG losses. Replace potassium (max 10-20 mEq/hr peripherally). Hypochloremia corrects with NS.

treatments.name: Serial abdominal examinations
treatments.result: Abdominal exam every 4-6 hours by surgical team. Watch for peritoneal signs, increasing distension, or worsening pain.
treatments.reasoning: CRITICAL for non-operative management. Development of peritoneal signs, fever, tachycardia, or increased pain = possible strangulation = go to OR.

treatments.name: DVT prophylaxis
treatments.result: Heparin 5000 units SQ q8h or enoxaparin 40mg SQ daily. SCDs applied.
treatments.reasoning: Hospitalized, immobile patient at high VTE risk. Pharmacologic prophylaxis unless contraindicated.

consults.name: General Surgery consultation
consults.result: Surgery consulted. Plan for trial of non-operative management with close observation. Will take to OR if develops strangulation signs or fails to improve in 48-72 hours.
consults.reasoning: Surgery MUST be involved in all SBO cases. Even if initially non-operative, 20-30% will require surgery. Surgical team follows patient.

disposition.name: Admit for non-operative management and observation
disposition.result: Admit to surgical service. NPO, NG decompression, IV fluids, serial exams, trend labs. Reassess in 24-48 hours.
disposition.reasoning: Partial SBO without strangulation can be managed non-operatively. Admit for close monitoring. 70-80% of adhesive SBO resolves with conservative management.

disposition.name: Operative intervention
disposition.result: Not immediately indicated - no strangulation. Would proceed to OR if: develops peritoneal signs, increasing lactate, no improvement in 48-72h, or Gastrografin doesn't reach colon.
disposition.reasoning: Indications for surgery: strangulation, complete obstruction not resolving, closed-loop on CT, incarcerated hernia, failure of conservative management after 48-72 hours.

actualDiagnosis.name: Partial Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction - Non-Strangulated
keyFindings: CT shows transition point without closed loop
teachingPoints: SBO mantra: 'Never let the sun set on a bowel obstruction' - but non-operative management can be tried for partial SBO without strangulation
Signs of strangulation: fever, peritoneal signs, elevated lactate, WBC >20K, severe constant pain
Initial management: NPO, NG tube, IV fluids, correct electrolytes, serial exams
Gastrografin challenge: if no resolution in 24-48h, oral contrast predicts need for surgery
Operate if: complete obstruction, strangulation signs, closed-loop on CT, no improvement in 48-72h
On surgery rotation, ALWAYS check hernia sites in any patient with N/V

female-72-sbo.teachingPoint: This patient can be initially managed non-operatively: NPO, NG tube to suction, aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, serial abdominal exams every 4-6 hours. The key question is: Is this partial or complete? Does she have strangulation? This patient has partial SBO (passing some gas) without strangulation (no peritoneal signs, normal lactate). Give her 24-48 hours to resolve with conservative management. If no improvement, she'll need surgery.
female-72-sbo.clinicalPearl: On surgery rounds, know the 4 cardinal features of SBO: (1) Colicky abdominal pain, (2) Vomiting (bilious if distal to ampulla), (3) Distension, (4) Obstipation. And know when to operate: (1) Signs of strangulation, (2) Complete obstruction that doesn't resolve, (3) Closed-loop on CT (surgical emergency). The attending will also ask: 'What's the difference between SBO and LBO?' Answer: SBO = bilious vomiting early, less distension; LBO = distension prominent, vomiting late or absent, obstipation more complete.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male arrives after a motorcycle accident with distended neck veins, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation
options.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.

options.text: Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
options.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Waiting for imaging delays life-saving treatment and can result in cardiovascular collapse and death. Treat first, image after.

options.text: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side
options.explanation: This classic triad (JVD, hypotension, absent breath sounds) is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE needle decompression before imaging. Per ATLS, insert a 14-gauge needle at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) followed by tube thoracostomy.
While Beck's triad (JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds) suggests cardiac tamponade, the ABSENT BREATH SOUNDS distinguish tension pneumothorax. Tamponade does not cause absent breath sounds.

female-52-mass.name: Charles Harris
female-52-mass.variantDescription: Breast mass workup - cancer vs benign

missedConsequences.Breast Cancer: Delayed diagnosis leads to higher stage at treatment, worse survival, more aggressive treatment needed.
missedConsequences.Inflammatory Breast Cancer: Aggressive subtype often mistaken for mastitis. Very poor prognosis if missed.

commonPitfalls: Reassuring patient that 'it's probably benign' before completing workup
Not doing complete breast exam including axillary nodes
Missing skin changes (peau d'orange, dimpling, retraction)
Not asking about nipple discharge
Forgetting to ask about family history including BRCA

mcqs.question: Which feature is MOST concerning for malignancy in a breast mass?

options.explanation: Mobile masses are more likely benign.
Most breast cancers are painless; painful masses are often cysts or fibrocystic changes.

options.text: Fixed, irregular mass with skin dimpling
options.explanation: Fixed to chest wall or skin, irregular borders, and skin changes (dimpling, retraction) are classic malignant features.
Bilateral often suggests fibrocystic changes (benign).

mcqs.question: What is the appropriate next step for a suspicious breast mass in a 52-year-old?

options.explanation: This is for younger women with likely fibrocystic changes. This patient is postmenopausal.
Triple assessment: clinical exam + imaging (mammo + US) + tissue diagnosis (biopsy if indicated).
MRI is for high-risk screening or extent of disease, not initial workup.
Never observe a suspicious mass - delays cancer diagnosis.

allDiagnoses.name: Invasive Ductal Carcinoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Age, postmenopausal, palpable mass with suspicious features.

allDiagnoses.name: Invasive Lobular Carcinoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Second most common breast cancer.

allDiagnoses.name: Fibroadenoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Benign tumor - but less common at this age.

allDiagnoses.name: Breast Cyst
allDiagnoses.rationale: Common, usually smooth and mobile.

allDiagnoses.name: Fat Necrosis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would expect history of trauma.

female-52-mass.oldcartsNarrative: "I found a lump in my left breast about 3 weeks ago in the shower. It's about the size of a grape, maybe bigger now. It doesn't hurt. It feels hard, not smooth. I've been scared to come in. My periods stopped about 2 years ago. My mom had breast cancer at 58 - she survived but had a mastectomy. My last mammogram was 3 years ago and was normal. I don't do self-exams regularly. No nipple discharge that I've noticed."

historyQuestions.text: How long have you had this lump?
historyQuestions.answer: About 3 weeks. Maybe a bit longer - I'm not sure when it first appeared since I don't check regularly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New, persistent mass in postmenopausal woman is cancer until proven otherwise.

historyQuestions.text: Has it changed in size?
historyQuestions.answer: I think it might be a little bigger. It's hard to tell.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Growing mass is concerning. Cysts can fluctuate; cancer typically grows.

historyQuestions.text: Is it painful?
historyQuestions.answer: No, it doesn't hurt at all. That's why I ignored it at first.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Most breast cancers are painless. Painful masses are more often cysts or fibrocystic changes.

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of breast or ovarian cancer?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, my mother had breast cancer at 58. She had a mastectomy and is still alive. I'm not sure about ovarian cancer in the family.
historyQuestions.reasoning: First-degree relative with breast cancer doubles her risk. Consider BRCA testing.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last menstrual period?
historyQuestions.answer: About 2 years ago. I'm in menopause.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Postmenopausal status means a new breast mass is more likely malignant.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last mammogram?
historyQuestions.answer: Three years ago. It was normal. I know I should have gotten another one.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Overdue for screening. Interval cancer can develop between screens.

historyQuestions.text: Any nipple discharge?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing coming out of my nipples.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bloody or spontaneous unilateral discharge is concerning for malignancy.

historyQuestions.text: Any skin changes on the breast?
historyQuestions.answer: I think there might be a slight dimple near the lump. The skin looks a little pulled in.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Skin dimpling/retraction suggests tumor tethering to Cooper's ligaments - malignant sign.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever taken hormone replacement therapy?
historyQuestions.answer: I took it for about a year for hot flashes but stopped.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Combined HRT increases breast cancer risk.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Vital signs normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: Breast masses don't typically affect vitals unless advanced disease.

physicalExam.finding: Asymmetry noted - left breast appears slightly distorted in upper outer quadrant. Subtle skin dimpling visible at 2 o'clock position left breast when arms raised. No nipple retraction. No peau d'orange. No skin erythema.
physicalExam.reasoning: CRITICAL: Skin dimpling with arm raising = tethering. Very concerning for malignancy.

physicalExam.finding: LEFT: 2.5cm firm, irregular mass at 2 o'clock position, 4cm from nipple. Poorly defined borders. Not freely mobile - somewhat fixed to underlying tissue. Non-tender. RIGHT: No masses, no dominant nodules.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic malignant features: firm, irregular, fixed, non-tender.

physicalExam.finding: No discharge expressed from either nipple. No nipple retraction or erosion.
physicalExam.reasoning: Paget's disease would show nipple eczema/erosion.

physicalExam.finding: LEFT: One firm, 1.5cm lymph node palpable in left axilla. Mobile. Non-tender. RIGHT: No palpable nodes.
physicalExam.reasoning: IMPORTANT: Palpable axillary node suggests possible lymph node metastasis. Will need biopsy.

physicalExam.finding: No supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.
physicalExam.reasoning: Supraclavicular nodes would indicate N3 disease (stage III).

imaging.name: Diagnostic Mammogram (bilateral)
imaging.result: LEFT: 2.8cm irregular, spiculated mass at 2 o'clock position with architectural distortion. Microcalcifications within mass. No other lesions. RIGHT: Scattered fibroglandular tissue, no suspicious findings. Assessment: BIRADS 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy, >95% probability).
imaging.reasoning: DIAGNOSTIC mammogram (not screening) for palpable mass. Bilateral to assess other breast. Spiculated mass with microcalcifications is CLASSIC for invasive carcinoma. BIRADS 5 mandates biopsy.

imaging.name: Breast Ultrasound
imaging.result: LEFT BREAST: 2.6 x 2.3 x 2.1 cm irregular hypoechoic mass with angular margins, posterior acoustic shadowing, and disruption of normal tissue planes. Not parallel to skin (taller than wide). LEFT AXILLA: 1.5cm lymph node with thickened cortex (>3mm), loss of fatty hilum, abnormal morphology. RIGHT AXILLA: Normal nodes.
imaging.reasoning: US characterizes mass (solid vs cystic), assesses features concerning for malignancy, evaluates axilla, and guides biopsy. Taller-than-wide orientation and posterior shadowing are concerning.

imaging.name: Breast MRI
imaging.result: Consider after biopsy confirms cancer to assess extent of disease, chest wall involvement, and contralateral breast
imaging.reasoning: MRI is most sensitive for extent of disease. Used for: surgical planning (breast conservation vs mastectomy), evaluating contralateral breast, occult primary with axillary mets. Not first-line imaging.

labs.name: CBC with differential
labs.reasoning: Baseline before treatment. Anemia may occur with advanced disease. Needed for chemotherapy planning.

labs.name: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Normal electrolytes, glucose 98. Calcium 9.4 (normal). LFTs normal: AST 24, ALT 28, Alk Phos 62, Tbili 0.8
labs.reasoning: Baseline liver and kidney function for treatment planning. Elevated calcium or liver enzymes would suggest metastatic disease (bone, liver mets).

labs.name: Tumor markers (CA 15-3, CA 27.29, CEA)
labs.result: Not routinely recommended for early-stage breast cancer per ASCO guidelines
labs.reasoning: Tumor markers NOT recommended for screening, diagnosis, or surveillance in early breast cancer. Low sensitivity, don't change management. May be used in metastatic setting.

procedures.name: Core Needle Biopsy (ultrasound-guided)
procedures.result: PATHOLOGY: Invasive ductal carcinoma, Nottingham grade 2 (tubule score 3, nuclear grade 2, mitotic rate 1). BIOMARKERS: ER positive 95% (strong), PR positive 80% (strong), HER2/neu negative (IHC 1+). Ki-67 proliferation index 15%.
procedures.reasoning: TISSUE DIAGNOSIS IS ESSENTIAL. Core biopsy (not FNA) provides architecture and biomarkers. ER/PR/HER2 status CRITICAL for treatment planning: ER+ = hormonal therapy, HER2+ = targeted therapy.

procedures.name: Axillary Lymph Node FNA or Core Biopsy
procedures.result: FNA of suspicious left axillary node: POSITIVE for metastatic carcinoma consistent with breast primary.
procedures.reasoning: Abnormal-appearing axillary nodes should be biopsied BEFORE surgery. Positive nodes = skip sentinel node biopsy, proceed to axillary dissection or neoadjuvant therapy.

procedures.name: BRCA genetic testing referral
procedures.result: Referred for genetic counseling given family history. Will test for BRCA1, BRCA2, and panel testing.
procedures.reasoning: First-degree relative with breast cancer warrants genetic testing. BRCA mutation affects surgical options (prophylactic mastectomy consideration), screening, and family counseling.

treatments.name: Staging workup before treatment planning
treatments.result: For clinical stage IIB (cT2N1M0): ASCO/NCCN recommend staging scans for symptomatic patients or N1+ disease. Order CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan.
treatments.reasoning: Staging workup (CT, bone scan, or PET/CT) for stage IIB and above. Rules out distant metastases before planning curative-intent treatment.

treatments.name: Multidisciplinary Tumor Board
treatments.result: Case presented at breast tumor board with surgical oncology, medical oncology, radiation oncology, pathology, and radiology.
treatments.reasoning: Multidisciplinary approach is standard of care. Team determines treatment sequence: neoadjuvant chemo vs surgery first, type of surgery, radiation, systemic therapy.

treatments.name: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy consideration
treatments.result: For this T2N1 tumor, neoadjuvant chemotherapy may allow breast conservation and assess tumor response. Anthracycline + taxane-based regimen likely.
treatments.reasoning: Neoadjuvant chemo for: larger tumors to allow BCT, node-positive disease, assess pathologic response (prognostic). This patient is candidate for neoadjuvant approach.

treatments.name: Surgical options discussion
treatments.result: Options: Breast-conserving surgery (lumpectomy + radiation) vs mastectomy ± reconstruction. Axillary management: sentinel node vs axillary dissection. Patient preference important.
treatments.reasoning: BCT + radiation has equivalent survival to mastectomy. Patient must understand options. Known positive node may require axillary dissection.

treatments.name: Adjuvant endocrine therapy (after surgery/chemo)
treatments.result: ER-positive tumor will require 5-10 years of hormonal therapy: Tamoxifen for premenopausal, aromatase inhibitor for postmenopausal.
treatments.reasoning: Endocrine therapy CRITICAL for ER+ tumors. Reduces recurrence by ~50%. Postmenopausal: anastrozole, letrozole, or exemestane. Premenopausal: tamoxifen ± ovarian suppression.

consults.name: Breast Surgical Oncology
consults.result: Surgical oncology consulted for operative planning and coordination of care
consults.reasoning: Surgical oncologist leads breast cancer care. Discusses surgical options, coordinates multidisciplinary treatment.

consults.name: Medical Oncology
consults.result: Medical oncology for chemotherapy planning - neoadjuvant vs adjuvant, regimen selection
consults.reasoning: Medical oncologist manages chemotherapy, hormonal therapy, and targeted therapy. Determines systemic treatment plan.

consults.name: Radiation Oncology
consults.result: Radiation oncology for post-operative radiation planning
consults.reasoning: Radiation indicated after BCT (always) and often after mastectomy (for node-positive or large tumors). Plan coordinated with surgery.

consults.name: Cancer Genetics
consults.result: Genetics counseling for BRCA and panel testing given family history
consults.reasoning: Genetic counseling assesses hereditary cancer risk, coordinates testing, provides family counseling.

consults.name: Plastic Surgery (if mastectomy)
consults.result: Plastic surgery consult for breast reconstruction options if mastectomy chosen
consults.reasoning: Reconstruction options: immediate vs delayed, implant vs autologous tissue. Patient should know options before surgery.

disposition.name: Outpatient oncology coordination
disposition.result: Outpatient management with expedited oncology appointments. Tumor board within 1-2 weeks. Treatment initiation within 2-4 weeks of diagnosis.
disposition.reasoning: Breast cancer is managed outpatient. Expedited workup and treatment initiation. Avoid delays beyond 4-6 weeks from diagnosis to treatment.

actualDiagnosis.name: Invasive Ductal Carcinoma, cT2N1M0, Stage IIB, ER+/PR+/HER2-
teachingPoints: Triple assessment for breast mass: Clinical exam + Imaging + Tissue diagnosis
BIRADS 5 = >95% chance of malignancy - must biopsy
ER/PR/HER2 status determines treatment: ER+ → hormonal therapy, HER2+ → trastuzumab
Clinical staging: T = tumor size, N = nodes, M = metastases
This patient (T2N1M0) will likely get neoadjuvant chemotherapy → surgery → radiation → hormonal therapy
Genetic counseling/BRCA testing given family history

revisedDifferential.name: Fibroadenoma
keyFeatures: Would be smooth, mobile, with benign imaging

female-52-mass.teachingPoint: This is a classic breast cancer presentation. On surgery/breast clinic rotation, remember: any new palpable mass in a postmenopausal woman is cancer until proven otherwise. Never reassure a patient until imaging and biopsy are complete. The triple assessment (clinical, imaging, pathology) must all agree before you can call something benign. Know your BI-RADS categories: 0 = incomplete, 1 = negative, 2 = benign, 3 = probably benign (short interval follow-up), 4 = suspicious (biopsy), 5 = highly suspicious (biopsy), 6 = known cancer.
female-52-mass.clinicalPearl: When presenting this patient on rounds, say: '52-year-old postmenopausal woman with a palpable left breast mass with concerning features - firm, irregular, fixed, with overlying skin dimpling and ipsilateral axillary lymphadenopathy. Family history significant for mother with breast cancer at 58. Diagnostic mammogram shows a 2.8cm spiculated mass, BIRADS 5. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma, ER/PR positive, HER2 negative.' The attending will ask about staging workup (CT chest/abd/pelvis, bone scan) and treatment plan.
