guidelineReferences.name: SAGES Guidelines for SBO Management
guidelineReferences.citation: Maung AA, et al. J Trauma Acute Care Surg. 2012;73:S362-S369
keyPoints: CT scan is imaging modality of choice
NGT decompression and IV fluids for initial management
Signs of strangulation require emergent surgery
Water-soluble contrast can be therapeutic and diagnostic

female-72-adhesive.name: Dorothy H.
female-72-adhesive.chiefComplaint: Severe stomach pain and vomiting for 2 days

missedConsequences.Small Bowel Obstruction: Untreated SBO leads to bowel ischemia, perforation, peritonitis, sepsis, and death. Time-sensitive diagnosis.
missedConsequences.Strangulated Obstruction: Compromised blood supply to bowel. Mortality increases significantly if surgery delayed >24-36 hours.
missedConsequences.Closed-Loop Obstruction: Both ends of bowel segment obstructed - cannot decompress, rapid progression to ischemia.

commonPitfalls: Missing the surgical history - prior abdominal surgery is #1 cause of SBO in developed countries
Assuming obstipation means complete obstruction - partial SBO can still pass some gas/stool initially
Not recognizing signs of strangulation (fever, tachycardia, localized tenderness, leukocytosis)
Relying on labs - lactate and WBC can be normal early in strangulation
Delaying surgical consultation while trying conservative management in complete obstruction

allDiagnoses.name: Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Prior abdominal surgery + classic SBO presentation (colicky pain, vomiting, distension, obstipation) = adhesive SBO until proven otherwise.

allDiagnoses.name: Partial Small Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: May be passing some gas - needs to determine complete vs partial for management.

allDiagnoses.name: Strangulated Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Bowel ischemia is surgical emergency. Look for: constant (not colicky) pain, fever, tachycardia, peritoneal signs, elevated lactate.

allDiagnoses.name: Closed-Loop Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Rapidly progresses to strangulation. CT may show 'C-sign' or 'beak sign' at two transition points.

allDiagnoses.name: Large Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Different management than SBO. Usually from cancer in elderly. Check for competent ileocecal valve (risk of perforation).

allDiagnoses.name: Ileus (Adynamic)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Non-mechanical - bowel just isn't moving. Would expect recent surgery, medications, or metabolic cause. No transition point on imaging.

allDiagnoses.name: Gastroenteritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have diarrhea, not obstipation. No distension. Different pain character.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pain out of proportion to exam early, then peritonitis. Usually atrial fibrillation or vascular disease. Different CT findings.

female-72-adhesive.oldcartsNarrative: "The pain started two days ago, comes in waves - really severe cramping then eases up some. It's all over my belly but worst in the middle. Probably 8 out of 10 during the cramps. Nothing helps. I've been vomiting - at first it was what I ate, then green stuff, now it's brownish and smells terrible. My belly is getting bigger and bigger. I haven't had a bowel movement in 3 days and I don't think I've passed gas either. I had a hysterectomy about 15 years ago. I've had this kind of thing before - once I was in the hospital for a few days with a blocked bowel but it got better without surgery."

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about any surgeries you've had.
historyQuestions.answer: I had a hysterectomy when I was about 57 - they took everything out. I also had my appendix out when I was young, and my gallbladder maybe 20 years ago.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL - prior abdominal surgery is the #1 cause of SBO in developed countries. Multiple surgeries = more adhesions.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned being hospitalized for a blocked bowel before - what happened?
historyQuestions.answer: That was about 5 years ago. They put a tube down my nose, I couldn't eat for a few days, then it opened up and I went home. They said I might need surgery someday if it happened again.
historyQuestions.reasoning: History of prior SBO that resolved with conservative management. Recurrent SBO is common with adhesive disease.

historyQuestions.text: Is the pain constant or does it come and go?
historyQuestions.answer: It comes in waves - really bad cramping for a minute or two, then eases up, then comes back. The waves are getting closer together though.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Colicky pain = mechanical obstruction (bowel trying to push past obstruction). Constant pain suggests strangulation.

historyQuestions.text: Describe the vomiting - what does it look like?
historyQuestions.answer: At first it was just food. Then it turned green. Now it's brownish and smells like... well, like stool. It's awful.
historyQuestions.reasoning: FECULENT VOMITING = late/distal SBO. Bilious (green) then feculent progression is classic. Indicates significant obstruction.

historyQuestions.text: When did you last pass gas or have a bowel movement?
historyQuestions.answer: I haven't had a BM in 3 days. Gas... I'm not sure, maybe a little yesterday? But definitely not today.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Complete obstruction = no flatus or BM. Partial obstruction may still pass some gas. Important for management decision.

historyQuestions.text: Has your belly changed in size?
historyQuestions.answer: It's getting bigger and bigger. My pants don't fit. It's uncomfortable to lie flat.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Progressive distension as bowel fills with air and fluid proximal to obstruction.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any fever or chills?
historyQuestions.answer: I felt a little warm this morning. Maybe some chills last night.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Fever with SBO is concerning for strangulation or perforation. Red flag.

historyQuestions.text: Have you ever been told you have a hernia?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I don't think so.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hernias are second most common cause of SBO. Must examine inguinal/femoral regions.

historyQuestions.text: Any history of cancer, especially abdominal or pelvic?
historyQuestions.answer: No cancer. My hysterectomy was for fibroids.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Malignancy can cause SBO from carcinomatosis or direct invasion.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

physicalExam.finding: BP 102/68 (low for her baseline of 130s), HR 108, RR 22, Temp 99.8°F, SpO2 96%
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia and relative hypotension suggest significant volume depletion from vomiting and third-spacing.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly woman in moderate distress, lying still, grimacing with waves of pain. Dry mucous membranes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Episodic distress corresponds to peristaltic waves against obstruction.

physicalExam.finding: Distended, tympanitic. Visible peristaltic waves. Well-healed lower midline surgical scar. No visible hernias.
physicalExam.reasoning: Distension and visible peristalsis are classic SBO findings. Surgical scar explains adhesive etiology.

physicalExam.finding: High-pitched, hyperactive bowel sounds with occasional rushes and tinkles.
physicalExam.reasoning: Hyperactive, high-pitched sounds = mechanical obstruction. Absent sounds would suggest ileus or late/ischemic obstruction.

physicalExam.finding: Diffusely tender but soft. No rebound tenderness. No guarding. No focal peritoneal signs.
physicalExam.reasoning: REASSURING - no peritoneal signs suggests no perforation or strangulation yet. This can change rapidly.

physicalExam.text: Examine for hernias (inguinal, femoral, umbilical, incisional)
physicalExam.finding: No inguinal, femoral, or umbilical hernias. Small incisional hernia at inferior aspect of midline scar - reducible, non-tender.
physicalExam.reasoning: MUST examine for hernias - second most common cause of SBO. This hernia is reducible, not the cause.

physicalExam.finding: Empty rectal vault. No masses. Guaiac negative.
physicalExam.reasoning: Empty rectum supports obstruction diagnosis. No blood makes malignancy less likely.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 13,200 (mild leukocytosis), Hgb 14.8 (hemoconcentrated from dehydration), Plt 310
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis can indicate strangulation but is nonspecific and unreliable. Elevated Hgb suggests hemoconcentration from significant volume depletion.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 132, K 3.1 (LOW), Cl 92 (LOW), CO2 30 (HIGH), BUN 38, Cr 1.4 (AKI from baseline 0.9), Glucose 145
labs.reasoning: Classic metabolic alkalosis from vomiting (losing HCl). Hypokalemia is common and needs replacement. AKI from dehydration.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.result: 1.8 mmol/L (normal <2.0). DOES NOT RULE OUT early strangulation.
labs.reasoning: Normal lactate is somewhat reassuring but CANNOT rule out early strangulation. Lactate rises LATE in ischemia. Don't be falsely reassured.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Rules out pancreatitis as cause of ileus.

labs.name: Type and Screen
labs.reasoning: Pre-operative preparation. May need blood products if surgery required.

labs.name: PT/INR/PTT
labs.reasoning: Pre-operative assessment.

imaging.name: Abdominal X-ray series (KUB, upright)
imaging.result: Multiple dilated loops of small bowel (>3cm) with air-fluid levels on upright film. Paucity of gas in colon and rectum. No free air under diaphragm.
imaging.reasoning: Classic SBO findings: dilated small bowel, multiple air-fluid levels, no colonic gas. Quick test to confirm clinical suspicion. No free air excludes perforation.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV contrast (GOLD STANDARD)
imaging.result: Dilated proximal small bowel up to 4.5cm with decompressed distal bowel. Clear transition point in right lower quadrant with adhesive band visible. Small bowel feces sign present. No pneumatosis intestinalis, no portal venous gas, no mesenteric haziness or free fluid to suggest ischemia. No closed-loop configuration.
imaging.reasoning: CT is GOLD STANDARD for SBO workup. Localizes transition point, identifies cause (adhesion vs hernia vs tumor), and evaluates for strangulation signs. Critical for surgical planning.

procedures.name: Nasogastric tube placement
procedures.result: 16 Fr NGT placed, immediate return of 800mL bilious fluid. Connected to low intermittent suction. Patient reports significant relief of nausea.
procedures.reasoning: NGT decompresses stomach, reduces vomiting and aspiration risk, may help partial SBO resolve. Document output color (bilious vs feculent) and quantity.

procedures.name: Foley catheter
procedures.result: Foley placed. Initial output 250mL concentrated urine.
procedures.reasoning: Strict I/O essential. Monitor urine output as marker of resuscitation adequacy. Target UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr.

procedures.name: Large-bore IV access x 2
procedures.reasoning: Large-bore access for aggressive fluid resuscitation.

treatments.name: NPO (nothing by mouth)
treatments.result: Strict NPO. All oral intake stopped.
treatments.reasoning: Bowel rest is mandatory. No oral intake until obstruction resolved.

treatments.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 2L NS bolus given, then maintenance at 150 mL/hr. Target UOP >0.5 mL/kg/hr.
treatments.reasoning: Patients with SBO are SEVERELY volume depleted from vomiting and third-spacing into bowel. Aggressive resuscitation is critical.

treatments.name: Potassium replacement
treatments.result: KCl 40 mEq IV in first liter, 20 mEq in subsequent liters. Recheck K in 4-6 hours.
treatments.reasoning: Hypokalemia from vomiting must be corrected. Follow levels closely.

treatments.name: NGT to low intermittent suction
treatments.result: NGT output 200-400 mL per shift. Bilious drainage.
treatments.reasoning: Continuous decompression. Track output quantity and character (bilious vs feculent).

treatments.name: DVT prophylaxis
treatments.result: SCDs applied. Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (hold if surgery anticipated within 12h).
treatments.reasoning: High DVT risk with SBO (immobility, dehydration, possible surgery). Mechanical and/or chemical prophylaxis.

treatments.name: Antibiotics (if strangulation concern or peritonitis)
treatments.result: Not started currently - no signs of strangulation. Would give broad-spectrum (piperacillin-tazobactam) if peritoneal signs develop.
treatments.reasoning: Antibiotics not routine for simple SBO. Give if: strangulation suspected, peritonitis, perforation, or pre-op for surgery.

treatments.name: Pain management
treatments.result: Morphine 2mg IV PRN for severe pain. Use judiciously - need to monitor for peritoneal signs.
treatments.reasoning: Pain control is appropriate but use judiciously. Don't mask peritoneal signs. Avoid NSAIDs (AKI risk).

consults.name: Surgery consult (MANDATORY)
consults.result: General surgery consulted emergently. Agree with trial of conservative management given no strangulation signs. Will follow closely. OR on standby.
consults.reasoning: Surgery MUST be involved in all SBO cases. Even with conservative management, surgery follows closely and is ready to operate if patient deteriorates.

disposition.name: Admit to surgery service
disposition.result: Admitted to general surgery service for SBO management. NPO, NGT, IVF, serial exams.
disposition.reasoning: All SBO patients require admission. Close monitoring for strangulation is essential.

disposition.name: Serial abdominal examinations
disposition.result: Abdominal exam every 4-6 hours by surgery team. Looking for peritoneal signs indicating strangulation.
disposition.reasoning: CRITICAL: Serial exams detect strangulation early. New peritoneal signs (rebound, guarding, rigidity) mandate immediate surgery.

disposition.name: Trial of conservative management
disposition.result: 48-72 hour trial of conservative management (NPO, NGT, IVF). Complete SBO without strangulation resolves 60-80% of time.
disposition.reasoning: Most adhesive SBO resolves with conservative management. Trial is appropriate if: no strangulation signs, stable vitals, improving symptoms.

disposition.name: Indications for operative intervention
disposition.result: Proceed to surgery if: peritoneal signs develop, fever/WBC rise, hemodynamic instability, CT signs of ischemia, failure to improve in 48-72h, complete obstruction not resolving.
disposition.reasoning: Know when to abandon conservative management. Delay in operating on strangulated bowel increases mortality significantly.

disposition.name: Diet advancement when resolved
disposition.result: When obstruction resolved: NGT output decreasing, passing flatus, pain improving → clamp NGT → if tolerated → clear liquids → advance as tolerated.
disposition.reasoning: Signs of resolution: decreased NGT output, return of bowel function (flatus/BM), pain improvement. Advance diet slowly.

disposition.name: Recurrence counseling
disposition.result: Counseled that adhesive SBO recurs in 20-30% of patients. May ultimately need surgery for recurrent episodes.
disposition.reasoning: Adhesive SBO tends to recur. Some surgeons recommend elective surgery after 2-3 episodes managed conservatively.

actualDiagnosis.name: Complete Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction (Non-Strangulated)
teachingPoints: CLASSIC SBO PRESENTATION: Colicky pain + vomiting + distension + obstipation (CVDO)
Adhesions from prior surgery are #1 cause of SBO in developed countries
COMPLETE vs PARTIAL: Complete = no flatus; Partial = passing some gas. Determines management urgency.
SIGNS OF STRANGULATION: Constant (not colicky) pain, fever, tachycardia, localized tenderness, peritoneal signs, elevated WBC/lactate
CT findings of ischemia: Pneumatosis intestinalis, portal venous gas, mesenteric haziness, bowel wall thickening, lack of enhancement
INITIAL MANAGEMENT: NPO, NGT decompression, IV fluids, correct electrolytes, Foley catheter
SURGERY INDICATIONS: Complete obstruction not improving in 24-48h, signs of strangulation, closed-loop obstruction
Most partial adhesive SBO resolves with conservative management (60-80%)

female-72-adhesive.teachingPoint: This patient has complete adhesive SBO - the most common cause of SBO in developed countries. Key management steps: (1) Make the patient NPO, (2) Place NGT for decompression, (3) Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation - these patients are very volume depleted from vomiting and third-spacing, (4) Correct electrolytes (watch potassium!), (5) Foley catheter to monitor output, (6) Serial abdominal exams to monitor for strangulation, (7) Surgical consultation. Because this appears to be a COMPLETE obstruction (no flatus) without signs of strangulation, she gets a trial of conservative management but with close monitoring. If she doesn't improve in 24-48 hours, or if she develops signs of strangulation, she needs surgery.
female-72-adhesive.clinicalPearl: The decision between operative and non-operative management of SBO is one of the most important clinical judgments in surgery. Red flags that mandate surgery: (1) Peritoneal signs - strangulation or perforation, (2) Closed-loop obstruction on CT, (3) Free air - perforation, (4) CT signs of ischemia (pneumatosis, portal venous gas), (5) Hemodynamic instability. The tricky part: lactate and WBC can be NORMAL early in strangulation. You cannot rely on labs to rule out ischemia. Clinical exam and CT findings are more important.

questions.stem: Which cognitive bias poses the greatest risk in this clinical scenario?

options.explanation: Premature closure — accepting a diagnosis before it is fully verified — is the single most common cognitive error in clinical reasoning across all settings.
Anchoring (fixating on initial impression) is a risk but can be mitigated by systematic differential generation.
Confirmation bias (seeking only supporting evidence) contributes to error but often follows from premature closure.
Availability bias (overweighting recent experiences) is common but not the most frequent source of error in this scenario.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male arrives after a motorcycle accident with distended neck veins, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation
options.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.
While Beck's triad (JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds) suggests cardiac tamponade, the ABSENT BREATH SOUNDS distinguish tension pneumothorax. Tamponade does not cause absent breath sounds.

options.text: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side
options.explanation: This classic triad (JVD, hypotension, absent breath sounds) is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE needle decompression before imaging. Per ATLS, insert a 14-gauge needle at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) followed by tube thoracostomy.

options.text: Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
options.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Waiting for imaging delays life-saving treatment and can result in cardiovascular collapse and death. Treat first, image after.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side.

guidelineReferences.name: Surviving Sepsis Campaign Guidelines 2021
guidelineReferences.citation: Evans L, et al. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143
keyPoints: Continuously reevaluate for alternative diagnoses in suspected sepsis (Best Practice)
If septic shock develops: antimicrobials within 1 hour (Strong)
Prompt removal of intravascular devices that are possible infection source (Best Practice)
Source control - address surgical site infection or abscess
30 mL/kg crystalloid for sepsis-induced hypoperfusion
VTE prophylaxis with LMWH unless contraindicated (Strong)

pod3-fever.name: Richard B.
pod3-fever.clinicalContext: 62-year-old man who underwent sigmoid colectomy for colon cancer 3 days ago. Has Foley catheter and peripheral IV.

timing.cause: Atelectasis - most common early post-op fever

allDiagnoses.name: Catheter-Associated UTI
allDiagnoses.rationale: POD 3, Foley in place, dysuria, cloudy urine - classic timing for 'Water'.

allDiagnoses.name: Atelectasis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can persist throughout post-op period.

allDiagnoses.name: Anastomotic Leak
allDiagnoses.rationale: After bowel surgery, leak = surgical emergency. Look for peritonitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Surgical Site Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually POD 5-7 but can be earlier. Examine wound.

allDiagnoses.name: Pulmonary Embolism
allDiagnoses.rationale: Risk increased post-op. Watch for hypoxia, tachycardia.

pod3-fever.oldcartsNarrative: [Nurse calls]: "Mr. B spiked a fever to 101.8. He had surgery 3 days ago. He passed gas yesterday and had a small bowel movement today. He says he has some burning when he pees. The Foley is still in."

historyQuestions.text: Any cough or shortness of breath?
historyQuestions.answer: I've been coughing a little. Using the incentive spirometer but it hurts my incision.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Assess respiratory cause. Poor IS compliance leads to atelectasis.

historyQuestions.text: Any burning with urination?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, it burns a little. The urine looked cloudy this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Burning and cloudy urine with Foley = likely UTI.

historyQuestions.text: How is your belly feeling?
historyQuestions.answer: Hurts where they cut me, but that's expected. No new pain.
historyQuestions.reasoning: New or worsening abdominal pain would suggest leak.

historyQuestions.text: Have you passed gas or had a bowel movement?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, passed gas yesterday, small stool this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Return of bowel function is reassuring against leak.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had any surgeries?
historyQuestions.answer: No surgeries.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Surgical history can explain symptoms and affect planning.

physicalExam.finding: Decreased breath sounds at left base. No crackles.
physicalExam.reasoning: Decreased breath sounds at base = likely atelectasis.

physicalExam.finding: Midline incision well-approximated. Mild perincisional erythema <1cm. No drainage or dehiscence.
physicalExam.reasoning: Wound appropriate for POD 3. No infection signs.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, appropriately tender. Bowel sounds present. No peritoneal signs.
physicalExam.reasoning: No peritonitis is reassuring. Leak would show peritoneal signs.

physicalExam.finding: Foley in place. Urine cloudy with sediment.
physicalExam.reasoning: Cloudy urine supports UTI. Foley should be removed.

labs.name: Urinalysis and Urine Culture
labs.result: UA: >50 WBC/hpf, positive nitrites, positive leukocyte esterase, many bacteria. Culture pending - likely E. coli.
labs.reasoning: CONFIRMS UTI. Pyuria + bacteriuria with Foley = CAUTI. Culture guides antibiotic de-escalation. Send BEFORE antibiotics if possible.

labs.name: CBC with Differential
labs.result: WBC 12,400 (mild leukocytosis), 80% neutrophils. Hgb 11.2 (expected post-op), Plt 310.
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis supports infection. Mild elevation consistent with UTI. High WBC (>15) or left shift would increase concern for serious infection.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 138, K 4.0, Cr 1.1 (baseline 0.9), Glucose 122. Mild AKI.
labs.reasoning: Baseline renal function for antibiotic dosing. Mild AKI common post-op. Monitor with treatment.

labs.name: Lactate (if concern for sepsis)
labs.reasoning: Normal lactate reassuring against sepsis. Would be elevated if progressing to severe infection.

labs.name: Blood Cultures x 2 (if high fever or sepsis concern)
labs.reasoning: Consider if temp >101.5°F or concern for bacteremia. UTI can seed bloodstream, especially with manipulation.

labs.name: Procalcitonin (optional)
labs.result: 0.4 ng/mL (low). Bacterial infection likely but not severe.
labs.reasoning: Can help distinguish bacterial from non-infectious fever. Low procalcitonin suggests uncomplicated infection.

imaging.name: Chest X-ray (portable)
imaging.result: Left lower lobe atelectasis. No consolidation, no effusion. Heart size normal.
imaging.reasoning: Atelectasis is most common cause of early post-op fever. Treat with incentive spirometry and ambulation. No pneumonia.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis (if concern for leak or abscess)
imaging.result: NOT indicated - patient has reassuring abdominal exam with return of bowel function.
imaging.reasoning: Would order if: worsening abdominal pain, peritoneal signs, failure to return bowel function, or persistent high fever despite treatment. Not needed here.

imaging.name: CT Chest (if PE suspected)
imaging.result: NOT indicated - no signs of PE currently.
imaging.reasoning: Would order if: hypoxia out of proportion, tachycardia, pleuritic chest pain, or high Well's score. Watch for DVT/PE especially after POD 7.

imaging.name: Lower Extremity Doppler (if DVT suspected)
imaging.result: NOT indicated currently. Patient ambulating.
imaging.reasoning: Screen for DVT if leg swelling, calf tenderness, or asymmetry. DVT/PE typically POD 7-10 but can occur earlier.

procedures.name: REMOVE FOLEY CATHETER (PRIMARY INTERVENTION)
procedures.result: Foley catheter removed. Patient voiding spontaneously.
procedures.reasoning: MOST IMPORTANT intervention for CAUTI. Remove catheter if not absolutely needed. Risk of UTI increases ~5% per day catheter is in place. Best prevention AND treatment.

procedures.name: Incentive Spirometry Reinforcement
procedures.result: Nurse educated patient on proper IS technique. Goal 10 times per hour while awake. Pain control optimized.
procedures.reasoning: Incentive spirometry treats and prevents atelectasis. Poor technique or inadequate use due to pain is common. Optimize pain control.

procedures.name: Early Ambulation Orders
procedures.result: PT consulted. Patient ambulated 50 feet x 2 today.
procedures.reasoning: Ambulation helps atelectasis, prevents DVT/PE, speeds recovery. Mobilize early and often.

treatments.name: Empiric Antibiotics for UTI
treatments.result: Ciprofloxacin 500mg PO BID x 7 days started. Will narrow based on culture.
treatments.reasoning: Empiric treatment for CAUTI. FQ or TMP-SMX if tolerating PO. Ceftriaxone if NPO or concern for resistant organism. Narrow based on culture and sensitivities.

treatments.name: IV Fluids (if indicated)
treatments.result: Maintenance fluids continued. Encouraging PO intake.
treatments.reasoning: Ensure adequate hydration. Transition to PO as tolerated.

treatments.name: Optimize Pain Control
treatments.result: Pain regimen optimized to allow IS use and ambulation. Multimodal approach: scheduled acetaminophen, PRN opioid.
treatments.reasoning: Adequate pain control allows deep breathing and ambulation - key for preventing atelectasis and DVT.

treatments.name: DVT Prophylaxis
treatments.result: Enoxaparin 40mg SQ daily continued. SCDs in place.
treatments.reasoning: DVT prophylaxis essential post-op. Continue until ambulatory. PE is later cause of post-op fever (POD 7+).

treatments.name: Acetaminophen PRN Fever
treatments.result: Acetaminophen 650mg q6h PRN temp >101°F.
treatments.reasoning: Symptomatic fever management. Also contributes to pain control.

consults.name: Notify Surgery Team
consults.result: Primary surgery team notified of fever. Reassured that exam is benign and UTI is likely cause.
consults.reasoning: Always communicate with primary team about post-op complications. They know surgical details and can assess wound.

consults.name: Infectious Disease (if complex)
consults.reasoning: ID consult for: MDR organisms, persistent bacteremia, immunocompromised patient, or failure to respond to appropriate antibiotics.

disposition.name: Continue Hospital Admission
disposition.result: Continue hospitalization for post-op recovery. Monitor fever curve and clinical response to antibiotics.
disposition.reasoning: Patient needs to complete post-op recovery. Monitor for response to UTI treatment.

disposition.name: Fever Monitoring Plan
disposition.result: Monitor fever curve. Expect improvement in 24-48 hours. Reassess if fever persists or worsens.
disposition.reasoning: Fever should improve within 24-48 hours of Foley removal and antibiotics. Persistent fever warrants additional workup.

disposition.name: Discharge Criteria
disposition.result: Discharge when: afebrile >24h, tolerating PO, pain controlled, bowel function returned, ambulating, wound appropriate.
disposition.reasoning: Standard post-op discharge criteria. UTI should not delay discharge if patient otherwise ready and on oral antibiotics.

actualDiagnosis.name: Catheter-Associated UTI + Atelectasis
teachingPoints: 5 W'S: Wind (POD 0-2), Water (POD 3-5), Wound (POD 5-7), Walking (POD 7+), Wonder drugs
FOLEY = UTI RISK: Remove ASAP. Risk increases ~5% per day.
ATELECTASIS: Most common cause of early post-op fever. Treat with incentive spirometry, ambulation.
ALWAYS EXAMINE: Look at wound, lungs, IV sites, Foley - don't just order tests.

pod3-fever.teachingPoint: POD 3 fever with Foley catheter = UTI until proven otherwise. Management: (1) Remove Foley - most important intervention, (2) Send UA/culture, (3) Start empiric antibiotics. Also encourage incentive spirometry for atelectasis. The abdominal exam showing no peritonitis is reassuring against anastomotic leak.
pod3-fever.clinicalPearl: The best way to prevent and treat catheter-associated UTI is to REMOVE THE CATHETER. Every day it stays in, UTI risk increases ~5%. Always ask: 'Does this patient still need the Foley?' If not, take it out.

reducible-indirect.name: Frank M.
reducible-indirect.chiefComplaint: Lump in my groin that comes and goes

herniaClassification.indirect: Through internal ring, lateral to inferior epigastric vessels. Follows inguinal canal. More common.
herniaClassification.direct: Through Hesselbach's triangle, medial to inferior epigastric vessels. Directly through floor.
herniaClassification.femoral: Below inguinal ligament. More common in women. High incarceration risk.

allDiagnoses.name: Indirect Inguinal Hernia (Reducible)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Groin bulge with cough impulse, reducible, follows inguinal canal.

allDiagnoses.name: Incarcerated Hernia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Non-reducible hernia is surgical urgency. Risk of strangulation.

allDiagnoses.name: Strangulated Hernia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Compromised blood supply = emergency. Look for severe pain, erythema, fever.

allDiagnoses.name: Femoral Hernia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Below inguinal ligament. High incarceration rate - often needs urgent repair.

allDiagnoses.name: Lymphadenopathy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Multiple, firm nodes. Different location and character.

allDiagnoses.name: Lipoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Soft, mobile, doesn't change with Valsalva.

allDiagnoses.name: Testicular Pathology
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can get above mass, in scrotum vs inguinal region.

reducible-indirect.oldcartsNarrative: "I noticed this lump in my right groin about 6 months ago. It comes and goes - it's worse when I'm on my feet all day or when I lift something heavy. When I lie down at night it goes away. It doesn't really hurt, just feels like a pressure or tugging sensation when it's out. I can push it back in myself. I've had to strain more with bowel movements lately. I work in construction. My dad had a hernia surgery when he was about my age."

historyQuestions.text: When did you first notice the bulge?
historyQuestions.answer: About 6 months ago. I thought it would go away but it's been getting bigger.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Gradual onset, progressive enlargement typical for hernia.

historyQuestions.text: Can you push it back in?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I can push it back in with my hand. When I lie down it goes back on its own.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL distinction. Reducible = less urgent. Incarcerated = urgent.

historyQuestions.text: What makes it come out?
historyQuestions.answer: When I lift heavy stuff at work, when I cough, when I strain to have a bowel movement. Standing all day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Increased intra-abdominal pressure causes hernia to protrude.

historyQuestions.text: Does it hurt?
historyQuestions.answer: Not really painful, more of an ache or pulling sensation. Nothing severe.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Severe pain would suggest incarceration or strangulation.

historyQuestions.text: Any nausea, vomiting, or inability to pass gas?
historyQuestions.answer: No, nothing like that.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Obstruction symptoms would indicate bowel incarceration - emergency.

historyQuestions.text: Any difficulty urinating?
historyQuestions.answer: No, peeing is fine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Large hernias can involve bladder, especially direct hernias.

historyQuestions.text: Any family history of hernias?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, my father had hernia surgery in his 50s.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hereditary component to hernia development (collagen disorders).

historyQuestions.text: What kind of work do you do?
historyQuestions.answer: Construction. Lots of heavy lifting.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heavy lifting increases intra-abdominal pressure, contributing to hernia.

historyQuestions.text: What medications are you currently taking?
historyQuestions.answer: Just blood pressure medicine.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Medications can cause symptoms and affect treatment choices.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any medication allergies?
historyQuestions.answer: No known drug allergies.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Critical for safe prescribing.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any other medical conditions?
historyQuestions.answer: High blood pressure. Otherwise healthy.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Past medical history affects diagnosis and treatment.

physicalExam.finding: Visible bulge in right inguinal region, more prominent with standing. Increases with cough/Valsalva.
physicalExam.reasoning: Must examine standing and with Valsalva to see hernia.

physicalExam.finding: Soft, non-tender bulge in right inguinal canal. Easily reducible with gentle pressure.
physicalExam.reasoning: Reducibility confirmed. Non-tender = not incarcerated/strangulated.

physicalExam.finding: With finger in inguinal canal, impulse felt on cough. Comes from lateral side (through internal ring).
physicalExam.reasoning: Lateral impulse = indirect (through internal ring). Medial = direct (Hesselbach's).

physicalExam.finding: Can get above the mass (not inguinoscrotal hernia). Testicles normal bilaterally.
physicalExam.reasoning: Must distinguish inguinal hernia from scrotal pathology. Can get above inguinal hernia.

physicalExam.finding: No bulge or impulse on left. Negative examination.
physicalExam.reasoning: Always examine both sides. ~20% of hernias are bilateral.

physicalExam.finding: No femoral bulge below inguinal ligament.
physicalExam.reasoning: Femoral hernias have high incarceration rate - important to identify.

labs.name: No labs needed for reducible hernia
labs.reasoning: Clinical diagnosis. Labs only if considering incarceration or strangulation.

imaging.name: Inguinal ultrasound (if diagnosis uncertain)
imaging.result: Not necessary here - clinical diagnosis clear
imaging.reasoning: US can confirm hernia if exam equivocal. Not routinely needed.

actualDiagnosis.name: Right Indirect Inguinal Hernia (Reducible)
keyFindings: No signs of obstruction or strangulation
teachingPoints: INDIRECT vs DIRECT: Indirect = through internal ring (lateral), follows inguinal canal. Direct = through Hesselbach's triangle (medial).
HESSELBACH'S TRIANGLE: Inferior epigastric vessels (lateral), rectus sheath (medial), inguinal ligament (inferior)
REDUCIBLE vs INCARCERATED: Reducible = can be pushed back. Incarcerated = cannot reduce = urgent.
STRANGULATION SIGNS: Severe pain, tenderness, erythema, fever, obstruction symptoms = EMERGENCY
FEMORAL HERNIA: Below inguinal ligament, more common in women, HIGH incarceration rate (40%)
MANAGEMENT: Reducible hernia = elective surgical repair. Incarcerated/strangulated = emergent surgery.
WATCHFUL WAITING: Option for minimally symptomatic hernias, but most progress and require eventual repair

reducible-indirect.teachingPoint: This patient has a classic indirect inguinal hernia - the most common type. Key exam findings: bulge that increases with cough/Valsalva, cough impulse from the lateral side (indicating passage through internal ring), easily reducible, and non-tender. Management: elective surgical repair (open or laparoscopic) when convenient. Counsel patient on signs of incarceration (unable to reduce, increasing pain) and strangulation (severe pain, vomiting, skin changes) which would require emergency surgery.
reducible-indirect.clinicalPearl: Anatomy matters: Indirect hernias pass through the internal ring, LATERAL to the inferior epigastric vessels, and follow the spermatic cord through the inguinal canal. Direct hernias push directly through the posterior wall (Hesselbach's triangle), MEDIAL to the inferior epigastrics. Femoral hernias are below the inguinal ligament and have the highest incarceration rate (~40%) - these should be repaired promptly even if asymptomatic.

guidelineReferences.name: Tokyo Guidelines 2018: Acute Cholangitis
guidelineReferences.citation: Kiriyama S, et al. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25(1):17-30
keyPoints: Charcot's triad: Fever, jaundice, RUQ pain (50-70% of cases)
Reynolds' pentad: Add hypotension + AMS = severe cholangitis
Grade I (mild): Responds to antibiotics alone
Grade II (moderate): No organ dysfunction but doesn't respond to initial treatment
Grade III (severe): Organ dysfunction - requires urgent biliary drainage
ERCP is primary drainage method; PTC if ERCP fails

female-68-choledocholithiasis.name: Margaret S.
female-68-choledocholithiasis.chiefComplaint: Severe belly pain, fever, and I look yellow
female-68-choledocholithiasis.variantDescription: Acute cholangitis from CBD stone - Tokyo Grade III (severe)

missedConsequences.Acute Cholangitis: Untreated cholangitis has mortality up to 50%. Requires urgent biliary drainage.
missedConsequences.Septic Shock: Biliary sepsis progresses rapidly. Source control (drainage) is essential.
missedConsequences.Hepatic Abscess: Untreated obstruction can lead to liver abscess formation.

commonPitfalls: Treating as simple cholecystitis - cholangitis requires biliary drainage
Delaying ERCP in severe cholangitis - this is an emergency
Not recognizing Reynolds' pentad (hypotension + AMS added to Charcot's triad)
Inadequate antibiotic coverage (need gram-negative and anaerobic coverage)
Missing the diagnosis when only 2 of 3 Charcot's triad present (only 50-70% have all 3)

mcqs.question: A 68-year-old woman presents with RUQ pain, fever 103°F, jaundice, BP 88/52, and confusion. Labs show WBC 22k, T.bili 8.4, ALP 450. What is the diagnosis and severity?

options.text: Acute cholecystitis - admit for IV antibiotics and cholecystectomy
options.explanation: Cholecystitis rarely causes jaundice or hypotension. This is biliary obstruction with sepsis.

options.text: Acute cholangitis, Tokyo Grade III (severe) - urgent ERCP needed
options.explanation: Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) PLUS hypotension and AMS = Reynolds' pentad = severe cholangitis. This patient has organ dysfunction and needs emergent biliary drainage.

options.text: Acute pancreatitis - NPO, IV fluids, pain control
options.explanation: Pancreatitis can coexist but jaundice with fever and hypotension indicates cholangitis requiring drainage.
Hepatitis doesn't cause this degree of hypotension, fever, and RUQ tenderness.

mcqs.question: What is the MOST important intervention for this patient with severe acute cholangitis?

options.text: IV antibiotics alone with close monitoring
options.explanation: Antibiotics are necessary but cannot treat the obstruction. Source control is required.
Cholecystectomy addresses the gallbladder, not the CBD obstruction causing cholangitis.

SOURCE CONTROL is essential in biliary sepsis. ERCP allows stone extraction, sphincterotomy, and/or stent placement to relieve obstruction. Should be done within 24h for Grade III, within 24-48h for Grade II.
There's no abscess mentioned. The issue is biliary obstruction requiring ERCP.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Cholangitis (Ascending)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) with biliary obstruction. Classic presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Choledocholithiasis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Most common cause of cholangitis. CBD stone causing obstruction and infection.

allDiagnoses.name: Septic Shock
allDiagnoses.rationale: Reynolds' pentad present (Charcot's + hypotension + AMS). Requires aggressive resuscitation and source control.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Pancreatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can coexist with cholangitis (gallstone pancreatitis). Check lipase.

allDiagnoses.name: Cholangiocarcinoma
allDiagnoses.rationale: Malignant obstruction can present similarly. ERCP will clarify.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Cholecystitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cholecystitis rarely causes jaundice. This is cholangitis (ductal infection).

allDiagnoses.name: Hepatic Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Usually more indolent presentation. Can be complication of untreated cholangitis.

female-68-choledocholithiasis.oldcartsNarrative: "The pain started yesterday in my upper belly, mostly on the right side. It came on gradually but got really bad overnight. I've had a fever since this morning - I felt so hot and was shaking. My daughter noticed my eyes look yellow. I feel confused and weak. I've had gallbladder attacks before but this is different - much worse. I feel really sick, like something is very wrong. I've barely been able to get out of bed."

historyQuestions.text: When did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: The belly pain started yesterday afternoon. The fever and yellow color started this morning. I've been getting worse all day.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acute onset with rapid progression typical of ascending cholangitis

historyQuestions.text: Have you had gallbladder problems before?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've had 'gallbladder attacks' a few times over the years - pain after fatty meals that goes away. Doctor said I had gallstones but I never had surgery.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Known gallstones = likely CBD stone causing obstruction and cholangitis

historyQuestions.text: When did you notice the yellow color?
historyQuestions.answer: My daughter noticed my eyes were yellow this morning. My urine has been very dark, like tea color.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Jaundice + dark urine = conjugated hyperbilirubinemia from obstruction

historyQuestions.text: What color are your bowel movements?
historyQuestions.answer: They've been pale, almost clay-colored the past couple days.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Acholic stools = no bilirubin reaching gut = complete biliary obstruction

historyQuestions.text: Have you felt confused or disoriented?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I've been feeling foggy and confused. I couldn't remember what day it was.
historyQuestions.reasoning: AMS = part of Reynolds' pentad = severe cholangitis. Indicates septic encephalopathy.

historyQuestions.text: Have you had shaking chills?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, terrible rigors. I was shaking uncontrollably this morning.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Rigors indicate bacteremia - bacteria entering bloodstream from infected biliary tree

physicalExam.finding: BP 88/52 (hypotensive), HR 118 (tachycardic), Temp 103.2°F (febrile), SpO2 94% on RA
physicalExam.reasoning: Hypotension + tachycardia = septic shock from biliary sepsis. Reynolds' pentad.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing elderly woman. Icteric sclerae. Confused, knows name but not date or location. Diaphoretic.
physicalExam.reasoning: Jaundice + AMS + toxic appearance = severe disease requiring emergent intervention

physicalExam.finding: RUQ tenderness with guarding. Positive Murphy's sign. No rebound. Bowel sounds present.
physicalExam.reasoning: RUQ tenderness classic for biliary pathology. No peritonitis (yet).

physicalExam.finding: Jaundiced skin and scleral icterus. No petechiae or purpura. Warm, slightly mottled extremities.
physicalExam.reasoning: Jaundice confirms biliary obstruction. Mottling suggests poor perfusion.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 22,400 with 88% neutrophils, left shift. Hgb 11.2. Plt 145 (low normal).
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis with left shift confirms infection/sepsis. Thrombocytopenia can indicate DIC.

labs.name: Comprehensive Metabolic Panel
labs.result: Na 134, K 3.8, Cr 1.8 (elevated), T.bili 8.4 (markedly elevated), D.bili 6.2, AST 245, ALT 312, ALP 456, Albumin 2.9
labs.reasoning: Obstructive pattern (elevated ALP, direct bili). Transaminases elevated from cholestasis. AKI from sepsis.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Mild elevation can occur with stone passage. Would be >3x ULN with true pancreatitis.

labs.name: PT/INR, PTT
labs.reasoning: Elevated INR from liver dysfunction/cholestasis (impaired vitamin K absorption). May need correction before procedure.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate confirms tissue hypoperfusion from sepsis. Poor prognostic sign.

labs.name: Blood cultures x2
labs.reasoning: Essential before antibiotics. Common organisms: E. coli, Klebsiella, Enterococcus, Bacteroides.

imaging.name: RUQ Ultrasound
imaging.result: Dilated CBD (12mm, normal <6mm). Multiple gallstones. Possible stone in distal CBD (shadowing). Gallbladder wall thickening.
imaging.reasoning: Confirms biliary obstruction. Dilated CBD with stones diagnostic. US is first-line imaging.

imaging.name: MRCP
imaging.result: May be helpful if diagnosis unclear, but should not delay ERCP in severe cholangitis
imaging.reasoning: MRCP can visualize CBD stones but delays definitive treatment. Not needed if US confirms diagnosis.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: Dilated biliary tree. CBD stone seen. No abscess. Mild peripancreatic stranding.
imaging.reasoning: Can confirm diagnosis and rule out complications (abscess, perforation) but shouldn't delay ERCP.

treatments.name: IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 2L lactated Ringer's bolus given. BP improved to 98/64.
treatments.reasoning: Septic shock requires aggressive fluid resuscitation. Target MAP ≥65.

treatments.name: Broad-spectrum antibiotics
treatments.result: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV (penicillin allergy noted - consider alternatives)
treatments.reasoning: Cover gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella), Enterococcus, and anaerobes. Given allergy, alternatives: Cipro + metronidazole or Aztreonam + metronidazole.

treatments.name: Emergent ERCP
treatments.result: GI consulted for emergent ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction
treatments.reasoning: SOURCE CONTROL. Grade III cholangitis requires drainage within 24 hours. ERCP is first-line.

treatments.name: Vitamin K
treatments.result: 10mg IV given for elevated INR before procedure
treatments.reasoning: Correct coagulopathy before ERCP to reduce bleeding risk from sphincterotomy.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Cholangitis, Tokyo Grade III (Severe) with Septic Shock
keyFindings: CHARCOT'S TRIAD: Fever (103.2°F), Jaundice (T.bili 8.4), RUQ pain
REYNOLDS' PENTAD: Add hypotension (88/52) and AMS = SEVERE cholangitis
Tokyo Grade III: Organ dysfunction (cardiovascular - shock, renal - AKI)
Cause: Choledocholithiasis - CBD stone on imaging
Elevated WBC, lactate, INR - markers of severe sepsis
teachingPoints: CHARCOT'S TRIAD: Fever + Jaundice + RUQ pain. Present in only 50-70% of cholangitis cases!
REYNOLDS' PENTAD: Charcot's + Hypotension + AMS = SEVERE, mortality up to 50% without drainage
TOKYO GRADING: Grade I (mild, responds to abx), Grade II (mod, no organ dysfunction), Grade III (severe, organ dysfunction)
TIMING OF DRAINAGE: Grade III = within 24h (emergent). Grade II = within 24-48h. Grade I = may try antibiotics first
ERCP IS FIRST-LINE: Sphincterotomy + stone extraction + stent placement. PTC (percutaneous) if ERCP fails
ANTIBIOTICS: Cover gram-negatives (E. coli, Klebsiella), Enterococcus, and anaerobes (Bacteroides)
COMMON REGIMENS: Pip-tazo, Ampicillin-sulbactam, or Cipro + metronidazole
CHOLANGITIS ≠ CHOLECYSTITIS: Cholangitis is DUCT infection (obstruction), cholecystitis is GALLBLADDER infection
COMPLICATIONS: Hepatic abscess, septic shock, secondary biliary cirrhosis if untreated

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Grade III cholangitis has mortality up to 50% without drainage vs <5% with timely intervention
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Kiriyama S et al., Tokyo Guidelines 2018
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Recognize severity early - Grade III requires emergent ERCP within 24 hours
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Mortality: Grade I 2.4%, Grade II 4.4%, Grade III 11.8% (with treatment); >50% without drainage

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Early ERCP (<24-48h) in severe cholangitis improves outcomes
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Khashab MA et al., Gastrointest Endosc 2016
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Do not delay ERCP for 'optimization' in severe cases - source control is priority
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Delayed ERCP (>48h) associated with increased ICU stay, mortality, and hospital costs

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Blood cultures positive in 40-70% of acute cholangitis
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Salek J et al., World J Gastroenterol 2009
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Always obtain blood cultures before antibiotics. Most common: E. coli, Klebsiella

female-68-choledocholithiasis.clinicalPearl: CHOLANGITIS IS A SURGICAL/GI EMERGENCY: Unlike cholecystitis which can often be treated with antibiotics and interval cholecystectomy, cholangitis requires BILIARY DRAINAGE. Antibiotics cannot penetrate an obstructed biliary tree. The key teaching point: Charcot's triad (fever, jaundice, RUQ pain) is present in only 50-70% of cases. When Reynolds' pentad is present (add hypotension and AMS), mortality approaches 50% without emergent drainage.

cases.title: Sudden Severe Abdominal Pain with Rigidity

guidelineReferences.name: WSES Guidelines for Management of Intra-Abdominal Infections 2017
guidelineReferences.citation: Sartelli M, et al. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:29
keyPoints: Perforated viscus requires emergent surgical intervention
Source control is priority - cannot treat with antibiotics alone
CT with oral/IV contrast is imaging of choice
Free air on imaging is diagnostic but absent in 20% of perforations
Broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-negatives and anaerobes

male-58-perforated-ulcer.name: Robert K.
male-58-perforated-ulcer.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe stomach pain like I was stabbed
male-58-perforated-ulcer.variantDescription: Perforated peptic ulcer with diffuse peritonitis

missedConsequences.Perforated Peptic Ulcer: Without surgery, mortality approaches 100%. Peritonitis leads to septic shock, multiorgan failure.
missedConsequences.Perforated Appendicitis: Delay increases morbidity. Abscess formation, prolonged recovery.
missedConsequences.Perforated Diverticulitis: Free perforation vs contained - determines surgical approach.

commonPitfalls: Delaying surgery for 'optimization' - source control is priority
Missing free air on supine X-ray - need upright or CT
Not recognizing the 'rigid abdomen' - classic peritonitis sign
Assuming patient with prior pain is having 'another episode'
Inadequate fluid resuscitation before OR

mcqs.question: A 58-year-old man presents with sudden severe epigastric pain radiating diffusely. Exam shows board-like abdominal rigidity, absent bowel sounds, and rebound tenderness. What is the BEST next step?

options.explanation: While CT can confirm, this patient has obvious peritonitis. Surgical consult should not be delayed for imaging.

options.text: Emergent surgical consult AND upright chest X-ray for free air
options.explanation: Peritoneal signs indicate surgical emergency. Get surgery involved immediately. Upright CXR can show free air under diaphragm quickly while preparing for OR. CT if diagnosis unclear.
This is NOT acid reflux. Board-like rigidity indicates peritonitis requiring surgery.

options.text: NG tube placement and admit for bowel rest
options.explanation: NG tube is appropriate but this patient needs surgery, not just supportive care.

allDiagnoses.name: Perforated Peptic Ulcer
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sudden severe epigastric pain, NSAID/alcohol use, peritoneal signs. Classic presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Diffuse Peritonitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Board-like rigidity, rebound, absent bowel sounds indicate generalized peritonitis.

allDiagnoses.name: Perforated Viscus (any cause)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Surgical emergency. Must identify source and operate.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Pancreatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can present with severe epigastric pain. Less likely to have board-like rigidity. Check lipase.

allDiagnoses.name: Ruptured AAA
allDiagnoses.rationale: In patients >50 with sudden abdominal pain and hypotension - must exclude.

allDiagnoses.name: Mesenteric Ischemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Pain out of proportion to exam early, then peritonitis when bowel necroses.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Gastritis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not cause peritoneal signs or board-like rigidity.

allDiagnoses.name: Biliary Colic
allDiagnoses.rationale: RUQ focused, not diffuse peritonitis.

male-58-perforated-ulcer.oldcartsNarrative: "It hit me like someone stabbed me in the stomach about 4 hours ago. I was just sitting watching TV and suddenly - bang - the worst pain of my life right in my upper belly. Now it's spread everywhere. I can barely move. Every breath hurts. I've had stomach problems before - burning after meals, taking ibuprofen every day for my back. I drink probably a six-pack most nights. I can't remember the last time I ate - I feel like I might vomit but nothing comes up."

historyQuestions.text: When and how did this start?
historyQuestions.answer: About 4 hours ago. I was just sitting there and suddenly felt like I was stabbed. The pain started in my upper stomach and now it's everywhere.
historyQuestions.reasoning: SUDDEN onset of severe pain is classic for perforation. Viscus ruptures at a moment in time.

historyQuestions.text: Do you take any pain medications regularly?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, I take ibuprofen every day for my back - probably 4-6 pills a day for years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic NSAID use is major risk factor for peptic ulcer disease and perforation

historyQuestions.text: How much alcohol do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: I drink beer every night. Usually 5-6 beers. Sometimes more on weekends.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heavy alcohol use combined with NSAIDs dramatically increases ulcer and perforation risk

historyQuestions.text: Any stomach problems before this?
historyQuestions.answer: Yeah, I've had burning in my stomach for months. Especially after eating. Never got it checked out.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Chronic dyspepsia/epigastric pain suggests pre-existing PUD now perforated

historyQuestions.text: Does moving make it worse?
historyQuestions.answer: Any movement is agony. I'm trying to lie completely still. Even breathing hurts.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Movement worsening pain = peritoneal irritation. Patient lies motionless (contrast with renal colic).

physicalExam.finding: BP 92/58 (hypotensive), HR 122 (tachycardic), RR 26 (tachypneic), Temp 101.4°F
physicalExam.reasoning: Septic shock physiology from peritonitis. Needs aggressive resuscitation.

physicalExam.finding: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic, lying completely still. Shallow breathing to minimize abdominal movement.
physicalExam.reasoning: Motionless positioning classic for peritonitis - any movement causes pain.

physicalExam.finding: BOARD-LIKE RIGIDITY - involuntary guarding diffusely. Diffuse tenderness. Rebound tenderness present. Absent bowel sounds.
physicalExam.reasoning: BOARD-LIKE RIGIDITY is pathognomonic for peritonitis. Involuntary guarding cannot be faked. Absent bowel sounds = ileus from peritonitis.

physicalExam.finding: Loss of liver dullness (tympany over liver area suggesting free air)
physicalExam.reasoning: Loss of liver dullness on percussion suggests free air between liver and abdominal wall

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 18,400 with 92% neutrophils, left shift. Hgb 14.2. Plt 198.
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis with left shift confirms significant infection/inflammation.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 138, K 3.4, Cl 102, CO2 18 (low - metabolic acidosis), BUN 32, Cr 1.8 (AKI from sepsis)
labs.reasoning: Metabolic acidosis and AKI from sepsis/hypovolemia. Needs aggressive resuscitation.

labs.name: Lipase
labs.reasoning: Normal lipase rules out pancreatitis as primary cause.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate confirms tissue hypoperfusion. Poor prognostic marker.

imaging.name: Upright Chest X-ray
imaging.result: Free air under right hemidiaphragm (pneumoperitoneum)
imaging.reasoning: Free air is DIAGNOSTIC of perforated viscus. Upright CXR is fastest test.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis with contrast
imaging.result: Large amount of free intraperitoneal air. Free fluid. Thickened proximal duodenum with adjacent inflammatory changes. Perforated duodenal ulcer confirmed.
imaging.reasoning: CT confirms diagnosis and may localize perforation site. But should not delay surgery if diagnosis clear.

treatments.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 3L LR bolus given. BP improved to 102/68.
treatments.reasoning: Septic shock requires aggressive fluid resuscitation before OR.

treatments.name: Broad-spectrum antibiotics
treatments.result: Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV given
treatments.reasoning: Cover gram-negatives, anaerobes. Give BEFORE OR.

treatments.name: Nasogastric tube
treatments.result: NGT placed to low intermittent suction. Coffee-ground aspirate.
treatments.reasoning: Decompresses stomach, reduces ongoing contamination.

treatments.name: Emergent exploratory laparotomy
treatments.result: Patient taken emergently to OR for exploration and repair
treatments.reasoning: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT. Source control cannot wait.

actualDiagnosis.name: Perforated Duodenal Ulcer with Diffuse Peritonitis and Sepsis
keyFindings: SUDDEN onset of severe epigastric pain - classic for perforation
Risk factors: chronic NSAID use + heavy alcohol = PUD
Absent bowel sounds = paralytic ileus from peritonitis
Free air on upright CXR = pneumoperitoneum = perforated viscus
Sepsis: hypotension, tachycardia, elevated lactate, AKI
teachingPoints: BOARD-LIKE RIGIDITY: Involuntary guarding that cannot be relaxed. PATHOGNOMONIC for peritonitis.
FREE AIR ON UPRIGHT CXR: Air rises - look under DIAPHRAGM. Present in ~80% of perforations.
LOSS OF LIVER DULLNESS: Free air between abdominal wall and liver causes tympany over liver.
SUDDEN ONSET: Perforation occurs at a moment in time. Patient can tell you exactly when it started.
LYING STILL: Peritonitis patients lie motionless. Renal colic patients writhe in pain.
PERFORATED PUD RISK FACTORS: NSAIDs, H. pylori, smoking, alcohol, stress, steroids
SURGERY IS MANDATORY: Cannot treat perforation with antibiotics alone. Source control required.
PREOP PREP: IV fluids, antibiotics, NG tube, Foley - but don't delay OR excessively

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: Timing of Surgery for Perforated Ulcer
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Buck DL et al., World J Surg 2013
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Operate as soon as patient is resuscitated - delay increases mortality

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Three factors predict mortality: shock, comorbidity, delayed presentation
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Boey J et al., Ann Surg 1987
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Boey score helps prognosticate: 0 factors = 0% mortality, 3 factors = 100%

male-58-perforated-ulcer.clinicalPearl: THE BOARD-LIKE ABDOMEN: When you feel true board-like rigidity - the abdomen is so tense it feels like a wooden board and the patient cannot relax it no matter how much you ask - you have peritonitis until proven otherwise. This is involuntary guarding from peritoneal irritation. Combined with sudden onset, absent bowel sounds, and free air on imaging, this is a surgical emergency. Don't wait for more tests - get surgery involved immediately.

cases.title: Painful Irreducible Groin Lump with Vomiting

guidelineReferences.name: European Hernia Society Guidelines on Groin Hernia 2018
keyPoints: Incarcerated hernia: cannot be reduced (surgical urgency)
Strangulated hernia: compromised blood supply (surgical emergency)
Signs of strangulation: severe pain, tenderness, erythema, fever, obstruction symptoms
Emergency surgery within 4-6 hours if strangulation suspected
Femoral hernias have highest incarceration rate (40%)

female-75-femoral-strangulated.name: Edith M.
female-75-femoral-strangulated.chiefComplaint: Painful lump in my groin and I've been vomiting all day
female-75-femoral-strangulated.variantDescription: Strangulated femoral hernia with small bowel obstruction

missedConsequences.Strangulated Hernia: Bowel necrosis occurs within 6 hours. Perforation, sepsis, and death if untreated.
missedConsequences.Small Bowel Obstruction: Complete obstruction leads to perforation, peritonitis.
missedConsequences.Femoral Hernia: 40% incarceration rate - much higher than inguinal. Must be repaired promptly.

commonPitfalls: Attempting aggressive manual reduction of a strangulated hernia - risk of reducing dead bowel
Mistaking femoral hernia for inguinal - femoral is BELOW inguinal ligament
Not recognizing obstruction symptoms as hernia-related
Delaying surgery while 'optimizing' patient - strangulated bowel doesn't wait
Assuming a small hernia is 'just a lymph node'

mcqs.question: A 75-year-old woman presents with a tender, erythematous, irreducible lump below the inguinal ligament with vomiting. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?

options.explanation: Location is KEY. Below inguinal ligament = femoral hernia, not inguinal.
BELOW inguinal ligament = femoral hernia. Tender, erythematous, irreducible with obstruction symptoms = STRANGULATED. This is a surgical emergency.
Lymph nodes don't cause obstruction. This has vomiting, suggesting bowel involvement.
Different location and presentation. Would have fever, hip pain, and gradual onset.

mcqs.question: What is the MOST important next step for this patient?

options.explanation: DANGEROUS. Strangulated bowel may be necrotic - reducing dead bowel into abdomen can cause perforation and peritonitis.
Diagnosis is clinical. CT may be helpful but should not delay surgery.

options.text: Emergent surgical consultation for operative repair
options.explanation: Strangulated hernia is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Bowel viability decreases hourly. Surgery should not be delayed.
Cannot observe a strangulated hernia. Bowel will die without surgery.

allDiagnoses.name: Strangulated Femoral Hernia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Tender, irreducible mass BELOW inguinal ligament with signs of bowel obstruction and strangulation (erythema, fever).

allDiagnoses.name: Small Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Vomiting, abdominal distension, obstipation - bowel incarcerated in hernia.

allDiagnoses.name: Strangulated Hernia
allDiagnoses.rationale: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Bowel necrosis occurs within hours.

allDiagnoses.name: Bowel Perforation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Complication of strangulated hernia if surgery delayed.

allDiagnoses.name: Incarcerated Inguinal Hernia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would be ABOVE inguinal ligament. This is femoral (below ligament).

allDiagnoses.name: Lymphadenopathy
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would not cause obstruction symptoms or be this tender.

female-75-femoral-strangulated.oldcartsNarrative: "I noticed a small lump in my right groin about a week ago. It was tender but I thought it would go away. Last night it got much bigger and very painful. I tried to push it back in but it won't go. I've been vomiting since this morning - at first food, now just green liquid. I haven't been able to pass gas or have a bowel movement since yesterday. The lump is getting more red and the pain is getting worse. I feel feverish."

historyQuestions.text: When did you first notice the lump?
historyQuestions.answer: About a week ago I felt a small bump. It came and went. But last night it got big and stuck.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Hernia that was previously reducible is now incarcerated - cannot reduce.

historyQuestions.text: Can you push it back in?
historyQuestions.answer: No, I tried but it won't go back. It's very painful to touch.
historyQuestions.reasoning: IRREDUCIBLE hernia = incarcerated. Combined with tenderness = concerning for strangulation.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about the vomiting.
historyQuestions.answer: Started this morning. First food, now just green liquid. Maybe 8-10 times today.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bilious vomiting indicates small bowel obstruction - bowel caught in hernia.

historyQuestions.text: When was your last bowel movement or gas?
historyQuestions.answer: Yesterday morning. Nothing since then. I can't pass gas either.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Obstipation (no stool or flatus) indicates complete mechanical obstruction.

historyQuestions.text: Has the skin over the lump changed?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, it's getting red. It was just skin-colored before.
historyQuestions.reasoning: ERYTHEMA over hernia is sign of strangulation - compromised blood supply.

physicalExam.finding: BP 108/72, HR 108 (tachycardic), Temp 100.8°F (low-grade fever)
physicalExam.reasoning: Tachycardia and fever suggest bowel compromise/early sepsis.

physicalExam.finding: 3cm tender, firm, erythematous mass in RIGHT groin, BELOW and LATERAL to pubic tubercle (below inguinal ligament). Overlying skin is red.
physicalExam.reasoning: Location is KEY: BELOW inguinal ligament = FEMORAL hernia. Erythema = strangulation.

physicalExam.finding: Extremely tender, firm, irreducible. No cough impulse. Patient winces with light touch.
physicalExam.reasoning: Tenderness + irreducibility + erythema = strangulation until proven otherwise.

physicalExam.finding: Distended, tympanitic. Mild diffuse tenderness without peritoneal signs yet. High-pitched bowel sounds with occasional rushes.
physicalExam.reasoning: Distension and hyperactive bowel sounds confirm mechanical obstruction.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 14,800 with left shift. Hgb 13.2. Plt 210.
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis suggests inflammatory response to strangulated bowel.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 134, K 3.2 (low from vomiting), Cl 98, CO2 20, BUN 28, Cr 1.3
labs.reasoning: Hypokalemia from vomiting. Mild AKI from dehydration.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Elevated lactate concerning for bowel ischemia.

imaging.name: CT Abdomen/Pelvis
imaging.result: Right femoral hernia containing small bowel loop with wall thickening and surrounding fat stranding. Proximal small bowel dilatation. Findings consistent with strangulated femoral hernia.
imaging.reasoning: CT confirms diagnosis and shows bowel involvement. But clinical diagnosis sufficient - don't delay surgery.

treatments.name: NPO, IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 2L LR given. Patient made NPO.
treatments.reasoning: Resuscitate while preparing for OR. No oral intake.

treatments.name: Nasogastric tube
treatments.result: NGT placed to low suction. 600mL bilious output.
treatments.reasoning: Decompresses obstructed bowel, prevents aspiration.

treatments.name: IV antibiotics
treatments.result: Cefazolin + metronidazole given
treatments.reasoning: Surgical prophylaxis. Cover for potential bowel contamination.

treatments.name: Emergent surgery
treatments.result: Emergent femoral hernia repair with possible bowel resection
treatments.reasoning: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT. Must assess bowel viability in OR.

actualDiagnosis.name: Strangulated Right Femoral Hernia with Small Bowel Obstruction
keyFindings: Mass BELOW inguinal ligament = FEMORAL hernia (not inguinal)
Tender, irreducible, erythematous = STRANGULATED (compromised blood supply)
Vomiting, distension, obstipation = Small Bowel Obstruction (bowel in hernia)
Tachycardia, fever, elevated WBC and lactate = early systemic response
Female patient - femoral hernias more common in women
teachingPoints: FEMORAL vs INGUINAL: Femoral is BELOW inguinal ligament, lateral to pubic tubercle. Inguinal is ABOVE.
FEMORAL HERNIA DANGER: 40% incarceration rate - MUCH higher than inguinal. All should be repaired.
INCARCERATED vs STRANGULATED: Incarcerated = irreducible. Strangulated = compromised blood supply (tenderness, erythema, fever).
DO NOT FORCEFULLY REDUCE: Risk of reducing necrotic bowel into abdomen = perforation.
FEMORAL HERNIA DEMOGRAPHICS: More common in women (anatomy: wider pelvis, larger femoral canal).
TIME IS BOWEL: Strangulation leads to necrosis within 6 hours. Surgery cannot wait.
RICHTER HERNIA: Only part of bowel wall incarcerated - can strangulate without complete obstruction.

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Femoral hernias have 40% incarceration rate vs 3% for inguinal
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Gallegos NC et al., Br J Surg 1991
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: ALL femoral hernias should be repaired electively - do not watch and wait
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Incarceration: Femoral 40%, Inguinal 3%. Strangulation: Femoral 20%, Inguinal 0.3%

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: Timing of Surgery for Strangulated Hernia
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Delay >6 hours from symptom onset significantly increases bowel resection rate
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Operate within 4-6 hours of strangulation symptoms to maximize bowel salvage
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Bowel resection needed: 13% if <6h vs 43% if >24h

female-75-femoral-strangulated.clinicalPearl: FEMORAL HERNIA PEARL: Location is everything. Inguinal hernias are ABOVE and MEDIAL to the pubic tubercle (through the inguinal canal). Femoral hernias are BELOW and LATERAL to the pubic tubercle (through the femoral canal). Femoral hernias are more common in women and have a MUCH higher incarceration rate (40% vs 3%). Any femoral hernia should be repaired promptly - even if asymptomatic. If you see an irreducible, tender, erythematous mass below the inguinal ligament, this is a strangulated femoral hernia until proven otherwise - call surgery immediately.
