cases.title: Severe Abdominal Pain Out of Proportion to Exam

guidelineReferences.name: ACG Clinical Guideline: Mesenteric Ischemia 2020
guidelineReferences.citation: Defined TL, et al. Am J Gastroenterol. 2020;115(8):1323-1339
keyPoints: Pain out of proportion to physical exam is classic early finding
CT angiography is imaging of choice
Arterial: SMA embolism (50%), SMA thrombosis (25%), NOMI (20%)
Venous: Mesenteric vein thrombosis (5-15%)
Lactate elevation is late finding - absence doesn't rule out
Emergent revascularization for arterial ischemia

female-78-sma-embolism.name: Dorothy W.
female-78-sma-embolism.chiefComplaint: Terrible belly pain - worst of my life - but doctor says my belly is soft
female-78-sma-embolism.variantDescription: Acute SMA embolism from atrial fibrillation

missedConsequences.Acute Mesenteric Ischemia: Without revascularization, mortality >80%. Bowel necrosis leads to perforation, sepsis, death.
missedConsequences.SMA Embolism: Cardiac emboli most common cause. AF is the source in most cases.
missedConsequences.Mesenteric Venous Thrombosis: More insidious onset. Still requires anticoagulation.

commonPitfalls: Reassured by 'soft abdomen' - classic early finding is pain OUT OF PROPORTION to exam
Waiting for lactate to rise - lactate is LATE finding when bowel is already dead
Not considering in AF patient with abdominal pain - cardiac emboli cause 50% of cases
Ordering CT without angiography phase - need CTA to see vessels
Delaying surgery for 'optimization' - bowel dies quickly

mcqs.question: A 78-year-old woman with atrial fibrillation presents with sudden severe abdominal pain. She is writhing in pain, but abdominal exam shows only mild tenderness without guarding. What is the MOST concerning diagnosis?

options.explanation: Pain out of proportion to exam is NOT gastroenteritis. This is the classic finding of mesenteric ischemia.

options.text: Acute mesenteric ischemia from SMA embolism
options.explanation: CLASSIC PRESENTATION: Severe pain out of proportion to physical exam in a patient with atrial fibrillation (embolic source). High mortality if missed.
PUD doesn't cause this severity of pain with benign exam. And wouldn't be related to AF.
IBS does not present with acute severe pain or in this clinical context.

mcqs.question: Initial lactate is 1.8 mmol/L (normal). Does this rule out mesenteric ischemia?

options.text: Yes - normal lactate excludes bowel ischemia
options.explanation: WRONG. Lactate is a LATE finding in mesenteric ischemia. By the time lactate rises, bowel is often necrotic.

options.text: No - lactate rises LATE and a normal value does not exclude early ischemia
options.explanation: Correct. Lactate rises only after significant bowel necrosis has occurred. Normal lactate early in course does NOT rule out mesenteric ischemia. CTA is needed.

options.text: Only if repeated in 6 hours and still normal
options.explanation: Cannot wait 6 hours - bowel may be dead by then. Need imaging now.

options.text: Yes, if combined with a normal WBC
options.explanation: WBC can also be normal early. Neither lactate nor WBC reliably exclude early ischemia.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia (SMA Embolism)
allDiagnoses.rationale: Sudden severe pain out of proportion to exam, AF (embolic source), elderly. Classic presentation.

allDiagnoses.name: Atrial Fibrillation with RVR
allDiagnoses.rationale: Known AF. Heart rate elevated. Source of emboli.

allDiagnoses.name: Mesenteric Ischemia
allDiagnoses.rationale: Mortality >80% if missed. Need CTA and emergent intervention.

allDiagnoses.name: Bowel Necrosis/Perforation
allDiagnoses.rationale: Late complication of ischemia. Peritonitis and sepsis develop.

allDiagnoses.name: Aortic Dissection
allDiagnoses.rationale: Can involve mesenteric vessels. CTA will evaluate.

allDiagnoses.name: Small Bowel Obstruction
allDiagnoses.rationale: Would have vomiting, distension, different exam findings.

allDiagnoses.name: Acute Pancreatitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Different quality pain, would have elevated lipase.

female-78-sma-embolism.oldcartsNarrative: "The pain started suddenly about 3 hours ago - like someone punched me in the stomach. It's all over my belly, the worst pain I've ever had. I can't get comfortable. But when the nurse pushed on my belly, she seemed surprised that it didn't hurt that much to touch. I have a heart condition - my heart beats irregularly. I take blood thinner but I ran out a few weeks ago and haven't refilled it. I've vomited twice and had diarrhea once - there was blood in it."

historyQuestions.text: When and how did the pain start?
historyQuestions.answer: Suddenly, about 3 hours ago. I was just sitting and it hit me like a punch to the gut. The worst pain of my life.
historyQuestions.reasoning: SUDDEN onset of severe pain is classic for arterial embolism. Thrombosis is more gradual.

historyQuestions.text: Do you have any heart problems?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, I have an irregular heartbeat - atrial fibrillation. Been on warfarin (Coumadin) for years.
historyQuestions.reasoning: AF is the SOURCE of emboli. 50% of acute mesenteric ischemia is from cardiac emboli.

historyQuestions.text: Are you taking your blood thinner?
historyQuestions.answer: I ran out about 3 weeks ago. I was going to get it refilled but kept forgetting.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CRITICAL: Stopped anticoagulation = formed clot in atrium = embolized to SMA.

historyQuestions.text: Any changes in your bowel movements?
historyQuestions.answer: I had diarrhea once and there was blood in it. Dark, maroon colored.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Bloody diarrhea ('currant jelly') is classic sign of bowel ischemia - mucosal sloughing.

historyQuestions.text: Any history of circulation problems or blood clots?
historyQuestions.answer: I had a small stroke a few years ago. They said it was from my heart rhythm.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Prior embolic event (stroke from AF) confirms high embolic risk.

physicalExam.finding: BP 148/88, HR 112 irregular (AF), RR 22, Temp 98.8°F
physicalExam.reasoning: Irregular heart rate confirms AF. Tachycardia from pain/stress.

physicalExam.finding: Elderly woman in severe distress, writhing, unable to find comfortable position. Appears very ill.
physicalExam.reasoning: Severe pain - but this is VISCERAL pain from ischemia, not peritoneal irritation yet.

physicalExam.finding: PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION TO EXAM: Patient reports 10/10 pain but abdomen is soft, only mildly tender diffusely. No guarding, no rebound. Bowel sounds present but diminished.
physicalExam.reasoning: CLASSIC FINDING: Severe pain with benign abdominal exam. Peritonitis develops LATE when bowel necroses.

physicalExam.finding: Irregularly irregular rhythm. No murmurs. S1/S2 normal.
physicalExam.reasoning: Confirms AF - the source of emboli.

physicalExam.finding: Maroon-colored stool on gloved finger. Guaiac strongly positive.
physicalExam.reasoning: Bloody stool confirms bowel mucosal ischemia/necrosis - 'currant jelly' stool.

labs.name: CBC
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis suggests ischemia/inflammation but can be normal early.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 140, K 4.8, CO2 18 (low - metabolic acidosis developing), Cr 1.4
labs.reasoning: Early metabolic acidosis from lactic acid production.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.result: 3.8 mmol/L (elevated) - NOTE: was 1.8 mmol/L initially and rose over 2 hours
labs.reasoning: Lactate elevation is LATE sign - bowel is dying. Normal lactate does NOT rule out early ischemia.

labs.name: PT/INR
labs.reasoning: Subtherapeutic INR confirms patient was not anticoagulated - allowed clot to form.

imaging.name: CT Angiography Abdomen
imaging.result: Filling defect in proximal SMA consistent with embolus. Decreased enhancement of jejunal and ileal loops. Small amount of free fluid. NO pneumatosis (bowel still viable).
imaging.reasoning: CTA is GOLD STANDARD. Shows SMA occlusion and assesses bowel viability.

treatments.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 2L LR bolus given
treatments.reasoning: Third-spacing and impending sepsis require aggressive fluids.

treatments.name: IV heparin anticoagulation
treatments.result: Heparin bolus and drip started
treatments.reasoning: Prevents clot propagation while preparing for intervention.

treatments.name: Broad-spectrum antibiotics
treatments.result: Piperacillin-tazobactam given
treatments.reasoning: Bacterial translocation occurs across ischemic bowel.

treatments.name: Emergent intervention (surgical or endovascular)
treatments.result: Vascular surgery and IR consulted emergently for revascularization
treatments.reasoning: REVASCULARIZATION is definitive treatment. Options: open embolectomy, endovascular thrombectomy/thrombolysis.

actualDiagnosis.name: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia - SMA Embolism from Atrial Fibrillation
keyFindings: PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION TO EXAM - classic early finding
Stopped anticoagulation 3 weeks ago = allowed clot formation
Sudden onset (embolic vs gradual onset in thrombosis)
teachingPoints: PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION TO EXAM: Severe pain but soft abdomen = MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA until proven otherwise
LACTATE IS LATE: Normal lactate does NOT rule out early ischemia. It rises when bowel is already dying.
CAUSES: Embolism (50%, usually from AF), Thrombosis (25%), NOMI (20%, in shock states), Venous (5-15%)
SMA EMBOLISM CLASSIC PATIENT: AF, recent cardiac event, or left-sided valve disease
CTA IS GOLD STANDARD: Must include arterial phase. Shows vessel occlusion and bowel viability.
SIGNS OF BOWEL DEATH: Pneumatosis, portal venous gas, free perforation - indicates non-viable bowel
BLOODY DIARRHEA: 'Currant jelly' stool occurs when mucosa sloughs from ischemia
TIME IS BOWEL: Irreversible damage occurs within 6 hours of complete occlusion
DON'T WAIT FOR PERITONITIS: By the time exam shows peritoneal signs, bowel is dead

evidenceBasedPearls.topic: Pain Out of Proportion to Exam
evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Classic early finding present in 75-95% of acute mesenteric ischemia cases
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Oldenburg WA et al., Arch Intern Med 2004; Kassahun WT et al., Int J Colorectal Dis 2008
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: When patient has severe pain but exam is surprisingly benign, think mesenteric ischemia
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Sensitivity of 'pain out of proportion' is 75-95% but specificity is low - must maintain high suspicion

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Untreated acute mesenteric ischemia has >80% mortality
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Bala M et al., World J Emerg Surg 2017
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Early diagnosis and intervention are critical - delays are deadly
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Overall mortality 60-80% even with treatment. Drops to 30% with early intervention (<24h)

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: CTA has sensitivity 94% and specificity 95% for acute mesenteric ischemia
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Defined TL et al., ACG Guidelines 2020
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: CTA is imaging of choice - must include arterial phase
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: CTA sensitivity 94%, specificity 95%. Superior to conventional angiography for diagnosis.

female-78-sma-embolism.clinicalPearl: THE CLASSIC TRIAD OF MESENTERIC ISCHEMIA: (1) Severe abdominal pain, (2) Pain out of proportion to physical exam (patient says 10/10 but abdomen is soft!), and (3) Rapid, unexpected intestinal emptying (vomiting and diarrhea). When an elderly patient with atrial fibrillation (or other embolic source) presents with sudden severe abdominal pain and a surprisingly benign abdominal exam, acute mesenteric ischemia should be at the top of your differential. Don't be reassured by a 'soft belly' - peritonitis only develops LATE when the bowel necroses. By then, it's often too late.

cases.title: Severe Perineal Pain with Swelling and Fever

guidelineReferences.name: WSES/GAIS Guidelines for Necrotizing Soft Tissue Infections 2018
guidelineReferences.citation: Sartelli M, et al. World J Emerg Surg. 2018;13:50
keyPoints: Fournier's gangrene is necrotizing fasciitis of perineum/genitalia
Surgical emergency - mortality 20-40% even with treatment
Risk factors: Diabetes, immunosuppression, obesity, alcohol
Polymicrobial infection (mixed aerobic and anaerobic)
Early aggressive surgical debridement is key to survival
LRINEC score helps identify necrotizing infection

male-62-diabetic.name: William T.
male-62-diabetic.chiefComplaint: Terrible pain in my private area with swelling and fever
male-62-diabetic.variantDescription: Fournier's gangrene in diabetic patient - surgical emergency

missedConsequences.Fournier's Gangrene: Mortality 20-40%. Each hour of delay increases mortality. Rapid tissue destruction.
missedConsequences.Necrotizing Fasciitis: Infection spreads along fascial planes at 2-3 cm/hour. Can destroy entire perineum.
missedConsequences.Septic Shock: Multi-organ failure and death without aggressive resuscitation and source control.

commonPitfalls: Mistaking for simple cellulitis or abscess - NSTI progresses much faster
Waiting for imaging to confirm diagnosis - delays lifesaving surgery
Inadequate debridement - must remove ALL necrotic tissue
Not recognizing pain out of proportion to visible findings - hallmark of NSTI
Underestimating severity in diabetics who may not mount typical inflammatory response

mcqs.question: A 62-year-old diabetic man presents with severe perineal pain, scrotal swelling with crepitus, and fever. What is the MOST important next step?

options.explanation: Do NOT delay surgery for imaging. Clinical diagnosis of Fournier's gangrene is sufficient - crepitus is pathognomonic for gas-forming infection.

options.text: Blood cultures and IV antibiotics, then reassess
options.explanation: Antibiotics alone cannot treat necrotizing fasciitis. Patient will die without surgery.

options.text: Emergent surgical debridement - call surgery NOW
options.explanation: Fournier's gangrene is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Mortality increases with each hour of delay. Surgery cannot wait for imaging or 'optimization'.

options.text: Incision and drainage of scrotal abscess
options.explanation: This is NOT a simple abscess - it's necrotizing fasciitis requiring WIDE debridement in the OR.

mcqs.question: Which finding is MOST specific for necrotizing soft tissue infection vs simple cellulitis?

options.explanation: Erythema is present in both cellulitis and NSTI.

options.text: Pain out of proportion to visible skin changes
options.explanation: Pain out of proportion + rapid progression + systemic toxicity = NSTI. The infection is DEEP in the fascia, so skin may look deceptively ok while fascia is necrotic.
Fever occurs in both conditions.
Both can cause swelling.

allDiagnoses.name: Fournier's Gangrene
allDiagnoses.rationale: Perineal/scrotal necrotizing fasciitis: severe pain, swelling with CREPITUS, systemic toxicity, diabetic risk factor.

allDiagnoses.name: Septic Shock
allDiagnoses.rationale: Hypotension, tachycardia, fever, elevated lactate from necrotizing infection.

allDiagnoses.name: Necrotizing Soft Tissue Infection
allDiagnoses.rationale: SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Mortality 20-40% with treatment, near 100% without.

allDiagnoses.name: Testicular Torsion
allDiagnoses.rationale: Acute scrotal pain - but no crepitus or systemic toxicity in torsion.

allDiagnoses.name: Simple Cellulitis
allDiagnoses.rationale: Cellulitis doesn't cause crepitus, necrosis, or this degree of systemic illness.

allDiagnoses.name: Scrotal Abscess
allDiagnoses.rationale: Abscess is localized. Fournier's spreads along fascial planes with systemic toxicity.

male-62-diabetic.oldcartsNarrative: "Two days ago I noticed some itching and pain near my bottom and privates. I thought it was just a rash. But it got worse fast - the pain is unbearable now, like my skin is on fire. My scrotum is swollen to twice its normal size. The skin looks dark in some areas and I can feel crackling under the skin. I've been feeling terrible - shaking, feverish, weak. I'm a diabetic but I don't take good care of it. I've been drinking more than I should."

historyQuestions.text: When did you first notice something was wrong?
historyQuestions.answer: Started 2 days ago with mild pain and itching. But in the last 24 hours it's gotten much, much worse. The pain is unbearable now.
historyQuestions.reasoning: RAPID PROGRESSION is hallmark of NSTI. Goes from minor to life-threatening in hours to days.

historyQuestions.text: You mentioned crackling under the skin?
historyQuestions.answer: Yes, when I touch around that area, I can feel and hear crackling, like bubble wrap.
historyQuestions.reasoning: CREPITUS is pathognomonic for gas-forming infection (Clostridium or polymicrobial).

historyQuestions.text: What does the skin look like?
historyQuestions.answer: It was red at first, now there are dark, almost black areas. The skin feels numb in some spots.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Skin necrosis (dark discoloration) and anesthesia (dead nerves) indicate advanced tissue death.

historyQuestions.text: Tell me about your diabetes.
historyQuestions.answer: I've had it for 15 years. My sugars run high - 300s, 400s. I don't take my medicine regularly.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Uncontrolled diabetes is #1 risk factor for Fournier's. Impairs immune response.

historyQuestions.text: How much do you drink?
historyQuestions.answer: A lot. Maybe a pint of vodka a day. I know it's bad.
historyQuestions.reasoning: Heavy alcohol use is major risk factor - immunosuppression and poor nutrition.

physicalExam.finding: BP 88/52 (SHOCK), HR 128, RR 26, Temp 103.8°F, SpO2 92% - patient is septic
physicalExam.reasoning: Severe sepsis/septic shock from necrotizing infection. Requires aggressive resuscitation.

physicalExam.finding: Toxic-appearing, diaphoretic, confused. In obvious distress.
physicalExam.reasoning: Altered mental status indicates septic encephalopathy - severe systemic infection.

physicalExam.finding: Scrotum massively edematous (3x normal size). Erythema extending to perineum and inner thighs. Areas of dusky/BLACK discoloration. CREPITUS palpable. Foul-smelling discharge. Skin anesthesia in necrotic areas.
physicalExam.reasoning: Classic findings: swelling, necrosis (black skin), CREPITUS (gas), and rapid spread. Anesthesia = nerve death.

physicalExam.finding: Erythema and induration extending to bilateral inner thighs, perineum, and lower abdominal wall. Infection spreading along fascial planes.
physicalExam.reasoning: NSTI spreads along fascia - must assess full extent for surgical planning.

labs.name: CBC
labs.result: WBC 28,400 (marked leukocytosis). Hgb 10.2 (anemia of chronic disease). Plt 98 (low - DIC developing).
labs.reasoning: Leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia (DIC) indicate severe infection.

labs.name: BMP
labs.result: Na 128, K 5.8, Glucose 482, Cr 2.8 (AKI), CO2 12 (severe metabolic acidosis)
labs.reasoning: Hyperglycemia (uncontrolled DM), AKI, acidosis - all poor prognostic signs.

labs.name: Lactate
labs.reasoning: Severe tissue hypoperfusion. Very poor prognosis.

labs.name: LRINEC Score
labs.result: Score = 10 (high risk for NSTI): WBC>25 (4pts), Hgb<11 (2pts), Na<135 (2pts), Cr>1.6 (2pts)
labs.reasoning: LRINEC ≥6 = high suspicion for NSTI. But clinical diagnosis shouldn't wait for score.

imaging.name: CT Pelvis (if time permits and won't delay surgery)
imaging.result: Gas tracking along fascial planes of scrotum, perineum, and bilateral thighs. Thickening and stranding of subcutaneous tissues. Consistent with necrotizing fasciitis.
imaging.reasoning: CT can show extent and gas, but SHOULD NOT DELAY SURGERY. Clinical diagnosis sufficient.

treatments.name: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
treatments.result: 4L crystalloid bolused. Norepinephrine started for persistent hypotension.
treatments.reasoning: Septic shock requires massive fluid resuscitation and often vasopressors.

treatments.name: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics
treatments.result: Vancomycin + Piperacillin-tazobactam + Clindamycin started
treatments.reasoning: Cover gram-positives (including MRSA), gram-negatives, AND anaerobes. Clindamycin for toxin suppression.

treatments.name: EMERGENT radical surgical debridement
treatments.result: Taken emergently to OR for wide debridement of all necrotic tissue. Multiple returns to OR planned.
treatments.reasoning: DEFINITIVE TREATMENT. Remove ALL dead tissue. Often requires multiple debridements. May need diverting colostomy.

treatments.name: Insulin drip for glucose control
treatments.result: Insulin drip started, target glucose 140-180
treatments.reasoning: Tight glucose control improves outcomes in diabetic sepsis.

actualDiagnosis.name: Fournier's Gangrene (Necrotizing Fasciitis of Perineum) with Septic Shock
keyFindings: RAPID PROGRESSION: Minor symptoms to life-threatening in 48 hours
CREPITUS: Gas in tissues = gas-forming organisms (polymicrobial)
SKIN NECROSIS: Dark/black discoloration, anesthesia (dead nerves)
SYSTEMIC TOXICITY: Septic shock, altered mental status
RISK FACTORS: Uncontrolled diabetes, heavy alcohol use
teachingPoints: FOURNIER'S = NECROTIZING FASCIITIS OF PERINEUM/GENITALIA - surgical emergency
MORTALITY: 20-40% even with treatment. 100% without surgery.
RAPID PROGRESSION: Infection spreads 2-3 cm/hour along fascial planes
CREPITUS IS PATHOGNOMONIC: Gas in tissues = necrotizing infection
PAIN OUT OF PROPORTION: Pain severe, but skin may look deceptively mild initially (infection is DEEP)
RISK FACTORS: Diabetes (#1), alcoholism, immunosuppression, obesity, malignancy
POLYMICROBIAL: Mixed aerobic + anaerobic bacteria. Requires broad coverage.
ANTIBIOTICS + SURGERY: Antibiotics alone will NOT work. Must debride all necrotic tissue.
REPEAT DEBRIDEMENTS: Usually need multiple trips to OR (second-look at 24-48h)
LRINEC SCORE: WBC, Hgb, Na, Glucose, Creatinine, CRP. ≥6 = high suspicion. But clinical diagnosis paramount.

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: Each hour of delay to surgery increases mortality by 7.6%
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Kobayashi L et al., J Trauma 2011
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Do NOT delay surgery for imaging or 'optimization' - operate immediately
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: Mortality: 27% if surgery <24h vs 47% if >24h. Each hour delay increases mortality 7.6%

evidenceBasedPearls.keyFinding: LRINEC ≥6 has PPV 92% and NPV 96% for necrotizing fasciitis
evidenceBasedPearls.source: Wong CH et al., Crit Care Med 2004
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: LRINEC helps identify NSTI but clinical suspicion should drive decision, not score
evidenceBasedPearls.statistic: LRINEC: ≥6 = high risk, 6-7 = intermediate, ≥8 = strongly suggestive of NSTI

evidenceBasedPearls.source: Laor E et al., J Urol 1995
evidenceBasedPearls.clinicalImplication: Higher FGSI = worse prognosis. Helps with counseling and resource allocation.

male-62-diabetic.clinicalPearl: THE 'HARD STOPS' OF FOURNIER'S GANGRENE: These findings mandate emergent surgery without delay: (1) Crepitus (gas in tissue), (2) Skin necrosis (gray/black discoloration), (3) Bullae with hemorrhagic fluid, (4) Pain out of proportion to visible changes, (5) Rapid progression (marked change in hours), (6) Septic shock with soft tissue infection. When you see these findings in the perineum/scrotum, the diagnosis is Fournier's gangrene until proven otherwise. Call surgery IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait for CT, do not wait for 'optimization,' do not pass go. Every hour of delay increases mortality.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old male arrives after a motorcycle accident with distended neck veins, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
options.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Waiting for imaging delays life-saving treatment and can result in cardiovascular collapse and death. Treat first, image after.
While Beck's triad (JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds) suggests cardiac tamponade, the ABSENT BREATH SOUNDS distinguish tension pneumothorax. Tamponade does not cause absent breath sounds.

options.text: Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation
options.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.

options.text: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side
options.explanation: This classic triad (JVD, hypotension, absent breath sounds) is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE needle decompression before imaging. Per ATLS, insert a 14-gauge needle at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) followed by tube thoracostomy.

preop-postop-primary.chiefComplaint: Patient with cardiac history needing non-cardiac surgery clearance
preop-postop-primary.variantDescription: Cardiac risk assessment, medication management perioperatively, VTE prophylaxis, post-op fever timing
keyFindings: Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI/Lee Index): 6 risk factors (IHD, CHF, CVD, DM on insulin, CKD, high-risk surgery)
Continue: beta-blockers, statins, antihypertensives (most); HOLD: ACEi/ARBs on day of surgery, anticoagulants per protocol
VTE prophylaxis: SCDs ± pharmacologic (heparin/enoxaparin) based on risk
Post-op fever timing: Wind (atelectasis, POD 1-2) → Water (UTI, POD 3-5) → Wound (SSI, POD 5-7) → Walking (DVT/PE, POD 5+) → Wonder drugs (drug fever)
